paper.title: Principal frequency of clamped plates on RCD(0,N) spaces: sharpness, rigidity and stability

global_context.setup: Let (X,d,m) be an RCD(0,N) metric measure space with N>1 and AVR_m >0. For an open bounded domain Ω ⊂ X, the principal frequency Λ_m(Ω) is defined as the infimum over H^{2,2}_0(Ω) of the Rayleigh quotient ∫(Δu)^2 dm / ∫ u^2 dm. The asymptotic volume ratio AVR_m is defined as lim_{r→∞} m(B_r(x))/(ω_N r^N).
global_context.target_theorem: There exists ε0>0 such that for every N ∈ (2-ε0,2+ε0) ∪ (3-ε0,3+ε0) and every open bounded domain Ω ⊂ X, one has Λ_m(Ω) ≥ AVR_m^{4/N} h_ν^4 (ω_N/m(Ω))^{4/N}, where ν = N/2 -1 and h_ν is the first positive root of the cross-product of Bessel functions J_ν and I_ν.

local_context: The proof of Theorem \ref{theorem-main} is inspired by the one of Ashbaugh and Benguria \cite{A-B-Duke}; however, several crucial differences appear that we explain in the sequel by outlining the argument. Let $u\in H_0^{2,2}(\Omega)$ be an eigenfunction to \eqref{Rayleigh}, whose existence is guaranteed by standard embedding arguments (see Proposition \ref{prop-inf-min}). Since we do not know a priori anything about the sign of $u$, we consider the subsets $\Omega_+=\{u>0\}$, $\Omega_-=\{u<0\}$ and $\Omega_0=\{u=0\}$ of $\Omega.$ Due to the lack of higher regularity of $u$ -- in fact, we only know that $u$ is locally Lipschitz, whose proof requires the dimensional restriction $N<4$ (see Proposition \ref{prop-lipschitz}), -- we shall restrict our analysis to the set $\tilde \Omega=\Omega_-\cup \Omega_+$\footnote{We thank Professor Mark Ashbaugh for suggesting this idea, which circumvents the regularity of the eigenfunction used in the smooth setting.}; this observation is in concordance incidentally with the fact that the unique continuation of harmonic functions on ${\sf RCD}(0,N)$ spaces generally fails whenever $N\geq 4$, see Deng and Zhao \cite{Deng-Zhao}. By using a suitable symmetrization on $\Omega_-$ and $\Omega_+$, we reduce our variational problem to a coupled ``two-balls problem" on the 1-dimensional model space $([0,\infty),{\sf d}_{\rm eu},\sigma_N=N\omega_Nr^{N-1}\mathcal L^1)$. To perform such a strategy, we explore three non-trivial features of ${\sf RCD}(0,N)$ spaces, namely: (a) the co-area formula (see Miranda \cite{Miranda}); (b) a particular form of the Gauss--Green formula (see Proposition \ref{prop-div-them} based on Bru\'e, Pasqualetto and Semola \cite{BPS-ASNS}); and (c) the sharp isoperimetric inequality which contains the constant ${\sf AVR}_ {\sf m}$ (see Balogh and Krist\'aly \cite{BK}).

In contrast to \cite{A-B-Duke} -- where the Euclidean space $\mathbb R^N$ has been considered for $N\in \{2,3\}$ -- we are interested in a wider range of not necessarily integers $N>1$ where the aforementioned ``two-balls problem" can be handled. Due to the lack of regularity, it is not likely that the approach of Leylekian \cite{Leylekian-ARMA} works for large values of $N$. paper.title: Global algebraic K‐theory

global_context.setup: The paper introduces parsummable categories: categories with a partially defined, strictly associative and commutative monoidal structure, algebras over the injection operad. For a parsummable category C, the global algebraic K-theory spectrum K_gl(C) is constructed as a symmetric spectrum. The paper also considers M-categories (enriched over the contractible groupoid M of self-injections of ω) and the free parsummable category P B generated by an M-category B. An M-category B is tame and without objects of empty support under certain conditions.
global_context.target_theorem: Let B be a tame M-category without objects with empty support. Then the symmetric spectrum K_gl(P B) is globally equivalent to the unreduced suspension spectrum of the I-space ρ(B).

paper.title: Semisimple groups interpretable in various valued fields

global_context.setup: We work in a tame valued field structure ℳ of one of three types: (1) power bounded T-convex expansions of o-minimal real closed fields, (2) V-minimal expansions of algebraically closed valued fields of equi-characteristic 0, or (3) p-adically closed fields (possibly with analytic expansions). These structures have geometric properties: dimension, generic differentiability, Taylor approximation, etc. Groups interpretable in ℳ are studied via four distinguished sorts: the valued field K, residue field k (if infinite), value group Γ, and the sort of closed 0-balls K/𝒪. A group is definably semisimple if it has no infinite definable normal abelian subgroups.
global_context.target_theorem: Let 𝒦 be either a power bounded T-convex field, a V-minimal field or a p-adically closed field. Let G be an interpretable definably semisimple group in 𝒦. Then there exists a finite normal subgroup N ⊴ G and two normal subgroups H₁, H₂ ⊴ G/N, such that (1) H₁ ∩ H₂ = {e}, H₁ and H₂ centralize each other and H₁·H₂ has finite index in G/N; (2) H₁ is definably isomorphic to a subgroup of GL_n(k); (3) H₂ is definably semisimple and definably isomorphic to a subgroup of GL_n(K).

paper.title: Koszul homomorphisms and universal resolutions in local algebra

global_context.setup: We consider finite local homomorphisms $\phi: (Q,\mathfrak{m}_Q,k) \to (R,\mathfrak{m}_R,\ell)$ of commutative noetherian local rings. The derived fiber is $F = R \otimes^{\mathbb{L}}_Q k$. $\phi$ is Koszul if $F$ is formal and $\text{Tor}^Q(R,k)$ is a Koszul $k$-algebra. $\phi$ is strictly Koszul if it is Koszul and the Koszul complex on a minimal generating set of $\mathfrak{m}_R$ is linear over a Koszul complex for $\mathfrak{m}_Q$ (Definition 2.4).
global_context.target_theorem: For any strictly Koszul local homomorphism $\phi \colon Q \to R$ there is a non-negatively graded, degreewise finite rank free $Q$-module $C$ such that: (1) For each finitely generated $R$-module $M$ with a minimal $Q$-free resolution $G\to M$, there is a differential $\partial^{\tau}$ on the graded $R$-module $R \otimes C \otimes G$ such that the resulting ``twisted tensor product'' complex $(R \otimes C\otimes G,\partial^\tau) \xrightarrow{\ \simeq\ } M$ is an $R$-free resolution of $M$. If $R$ and $M$ have finite projective dimension over $Q$, then both $C$ and the twisted tensor product differential can be explicitly described in their entirety with a finite amount of data. (2) Assume that $\phi$ is small (a central case of interest is $(Q,\fm_Q)$ regular and $\ker(\phi)\subseteq \fm^2_Q$). The twisted tensor product complex is minimal for the residue field $k$ of $R$. More generally, the resolution is minimal whenever $M$ is inert with respect to $\phi$, in the sense of Lescot. In particular, $\sum_i \rank_Q(C_i)t^i = \frac{ \ps^R_k(t)}{P^Q_k(t)}$.

local_context: Part \cref{introitem1} of the \namecref{intro:transfer}, with an explicit description of the twisted tensor product differential, is \cref{priddy-resolution}, while part \cref{introitem2} is contained in \cref{t_inert}. The examples \cref{introex1,introex2,introex3,introex4}, and several more, are introduced in \cref{sec:examples-koszul} and treated again in \cref{sec:examples-special-koszul}, with a complete description of the corresponding $C$ in each case.

Universal resolutions, that is, free resolutions over a ring that are defined in a uniform way for all finitely generated modules, have been of central interest in homological commutative algebra since at least the 60s, often importing tools such as Massey operations and bar resolutions from algebraic topology.

Let $\phi\colon Q \to R$ be a local homomorphism. Shamash constructed universal resolutions for $R$-modules when $\phi$ is a hypersurface quotient \cite{Shamash:1969}, and these were clarified and extended to complete intersection quotients by Eisenbud using the theory of higher homotopies \cite{Eisenbud:1980}. global_context.target_theorem: Let X be a smooth projective Calabi–Yau 3-fold, and let F be an ω-stable torsion free sheaf on X of rank r and homological dimension at most one. Then DDT_F(q) = M((-1)^r q)^{r χ(X)} · PPT_F(q).

paper.title: Twisted sheaves and $\mathrm{SU}(r) / \mathbb{Z}_r$ Vafa-Witten theory

global_context.setup: Let S be a smooth projective surface with H_1(S,Z)=0, r a prime number, and c_1 ∈ H^2(S,Z). The SU(r) Vafa-Witten partition function Z^{SU(r)}_{c_1}(q) is defined via virtual localization on moduli spaces of stable Higgs pairs. The SU(r)/Z_r partition function Z^{SU(r)/Z_r}_{c_1}(q) is defined using moduli spaces of α-twisted sheaves, where α is a Brauer class corresponding to w ∈ H^2(S,μ_r), using de Jong's period-index theorem to ensure existence of a P^{r-1}-bundle Y → S with w(Y)=w.
global_context.target_theorem: For any K3 surface S and prime number r, the S-duality conjecture holds: Z^{SU(r)}_{c_1}(-1/τ) = (-1)^{(r-1)χ(O_S)} (rτ/i)^{-e(S)/2} Z^{SU(r)/Z_r}_{c_1}(τ).

local_context: Consider the short exact sequences \begin{align} \begin{split} \label{ses} 1 &\rightarrow \O_S^* \rightarrow \mathrm{GL}(r) \rightarrow \mathrm{PGL}(r) \rightarrow 1, \\ 1 &\rightarrow \mu_r \rightarrow \mathrm{SL}(r) \rightarrow \mathrm{PGL}(r) \rightarrow 1. \end{split} \end{align} From these sequences and the inclusion $\mu_r \leq \O_S^*$, we obtain (commuting) maps \begin{align*} H^1(S,\mathrm{PGL}(r)) \stackrel{\delta}{\rightarrow} H^2(S,\O_S^*), \quad H^1(S,\mathrm{PGL}(r)) \stackrel{\delta'}{\rightarrow} H^2(S,\mu_r), \quad H^2(S,\mu_r) \stackrel{o}{\rightarrow} H^2(S,\O_S^*). \end{align*} Fix a class $w \in H^2(S,\mu_r)$. An important consequence of the period-index theorem of A.J.~de Jong is that $\delta'$ is \emph{surjective} \cite[Cor.~4.2.2.4]{Lie2}, \cite{dJo}. For $S$ a $K3$ surface, surjectivity of $\delta'$ was proved in \cite{HSc}. Hence we can pick a $\PP^{r-1}$-bundle $ p : Y \rightarrow S $ such that $w(Y) := \delta'([Y]) = w$. Our construction will be independent of the choice of $p : Y \rightarrow S$ satisfying $w(Y) = w$. Applying $o$, we obtain a Brauer class $$ \alpha := o(w) = \delta([Y]) \in H^2(S,\O_S^*)_{\tor}, $$ where $H^2(S,\O_S^*)_{\tor}$ denotes the torsion part of $H^2(S,\O_S^*)$, which is isomorphic to the Brauer group $\mathrm{Br}(S)$ \cite{Gro}.

Let $\Coh(S,\alpha)$ be the category of $\alpha$-twisted sheaves on $S$ \cite{Cal}. Using an analytic trivialization $\{U_i\}_i$ of $p : Y \rightarrow S$, locally $Y_i:=p^{-1}(U_i) \cong \PP^{r-1} \times U_i$ has a tautological line bundle $\O_{Y_i}(\lambda_i)$. These line bundles glue to

paper.title: Uniform Yomdin–Gromov parametrizations andpoints of bounded height in valued fields

global_context.setup: We work with subanalytic sets over non-Archimedean local fields L (e.g., Q_p or F_q((t))) with valuation ring O_L. The language includes subanalytic structure, and we add algebraic Skolem functions in the residue field to achieve that definable closure equals algebraic closure.
global_context.target_theorem: Let X be a subanalytic set included in some cartesian power of the valuation ring, and of dimension d. Then there exist integers C and N such that if L is a local field of residue characteristic p_L ≥ N, then for each integer r>0, there is a partition of X(L) into C r^d pieces such that for each piece X_i, there is a surjective function f_i : U_i ⊆ O_L^d → X_i satisfying T_r-approximation on U_i.

local_context: We state a uniform version of the Cluckers-Comte-Loeser non-Archimedean point counting theorem. A semi-algebraic set is a set defined by a first order formula in the language $\cL_\mathrm{div}=\set{0,1,+,\cdot,\mid}$ and parameters in $\ZZ\llb t\rrb$, where $\mid$ is a relation interpreted by $x\mid y$ if and only if $\ord(y)\leq \ord(x)$, with $\ord$ the valu\-ation. As usual, we will identify definable sets with the formulas that define them. Subanalytic sets are definable sets in the language obtained by adding a new symbol for each analytic function with coefficients in $\ZZ\llb t\rrb$ to the language $\cL_\mathrm{div}$. For each local field $L$ of characteristic zero, we fix a choice of uniformizer $\varpi_L$ and view it as a $\ZZ\llb t \rrb$-ring by sending $t$ to $\varphi_L$. Hence, we can consider the $L$-points of a semi-algebraic or subanalytic set, for $L$ a local field of any characteristic. The notion of semi-algebraic and subanalytic sets considered in Section \ref{section_PiWi} is slightly more general than the one considered here, see also Setting \ref{setting-T_DP}.

The dimension of a subanalytic set $X$ is the largest $d$ such that there exists a coordinate projection $p$ to a linear space of dimension $d$ such that $p(X)$ contains an open ball. A subanalytic set is said to be of pure dimension $d$ if for each $x\in X$ and every ball $B$ centered at $x$, $X\cap B$ is of dimension $d$. If $X\subseteq L^n$, we denote by $X^\alg$ the union of all semi-algebraic curves of pure dimension 1 contained in $X$. Observe that in general, $X^\alg$ is not semi-algebraic (nor subanalytic).

If $X\subseteq K^m$ and $H\geq 1$, with $K$ a field of characteristic zero, we denote by $X(\QQ,H)$ the set of $x=(x_1,\dots,x_m)\in X\cap \QQ^m$ that can be written as $x_i=a_i/b_i$, with $a_i,b_i\in \ZZ$, $\abs{a_i},\abs{b_i}\leq H$ (where $\abs{\cdot}$ is the Archimedean absolute value). If $X\subseteq L^m$, where $L=\FF_q\llp t\rrp$, we denote by $X(\FF_q(t),H)$ the set of $x=(x_1,\dots,x_m)\in X\cap \FF_q(t)^m$ that can be

paper.title: Realizability and tameness of fusion systems

global_context.setup: Saturated fusion systems over finite p-groups, components, realizable fusion systems, normal fusion subsystems. For a prime p, a saturated fusion system F over a finite p-group S is realized by a finite group G if S is a Sylow p-subgroup of G and morphisms in F are induced by conjugation in G. A component is a subnormal quasisimple fusion subsystem. The generalized Fitting subsystem is the central product of components and O_p(F).
global_context.target_theorem: Let p be a prime, let F be a saturated fusion system over a finite p-group, and let E be a normal fusion subsystem that contains all components of F. If E is realizable, then F is also realizable.

paper.title: Stability conditions for line bundles on nodal curves

global_context.setup: Let X be a nodal curve. A smoothable fine compactified Jacobian is a connected, open, proper subspace of the moduli space of rank 1 torsion-free simple sheaves of fixed degree on X, satisfying a smoothability condition relative to a regular smoothing. A stability assignment is a combinatorial rule assigning stability to multidegrees and subsets of components of X.
global_context.target_theorem: Taking the associated assignment induces a bijection between smoothable fine compactified Jacobians of X and stability assignments for X as introduced in Definition~\ref{finejacstab}.

paper.title: Combining rational maps and Kleinian groups via orbit equivalence

global_context.setup: The paper develops a framework for mating Kleinian groups with rational maps. It considers finitely generated non-elementary Kleinian groups in the closure of a Bers slice of a Fuchsian lattice, and Bers rational maps (rational maps with a simply connected completely invariant Fatou component). The mating aims to combine the dynamics via a dynamically natural homeomorphism between the limit set and the Julia set.
global_context.target_theorem: The only torsion-free Fuchsian groups that can be mated with rational maps in this framework are punctured sphere groups.

Then $\pmb{\Phi}(\partial\mathcal{U})$ is the boundary of an equatorial principal component of $\Omega(\Gamma)$, and hence $A_{\Gamma, \mathrm{BS}}:\pmb{\Phi}(\partial\mathcal{U})\to\pmb{\Phi}(\partial\mathcal{U})$ is a covering of some degree $k\geq 2$ (by part (1) of Theorem~\ref{first_return_conf_model_thm}). Therefore, $P_\Gamma:\partial\mathcal{U}\to\partial\mathcal{U}$ is a covering map of degree $k\geq 2$, so $P_\Gamma:\mathcal{U}\to\mathcal{U}$ is a proper branched covering of degree $k$. Choose (the homeomorphic extension of) a Riemann uniformization $\phi_{\mathcal{U}}:\overline{\D}\to\overline{\mathcal{U}}$ that sends $0$ to a critical point of $P_\Gamma$. Then, $\phi_{\mathcal{U}}^{-1}\circ P_\Gamma\circ \phi_{\mathcal{U}}$ is a degree $k$ proper branched covering of $\D$, and hence a Blaschke product $B$ of degree $k$. Moreover, postcritical finiteness of $P_\Gamma$ implies postcritical finiteness of $B$.

paper.title: Counting abelian varieties over finite fields via Frobenius densities

global_context.setup: Let [X,λ] be a principally polarized abelian variety over a finite field F_q with commutative endomorphism ring; suppose either X is ordinary or F_q = F_p is prime. The weighted cardinality of its isogeny class is denoted #~I([X,λ],F_q).
global_context.target_theorem: Let [X,λ] be a principally polarized abelian variety over F_q with commutative endomorphism ring. Suppose that either X is ordinary or that F_q = F_p is the prime field. Then #~I([X,λ],F_q) = q^{dim(A_g)/2} τ_T ν_∞([X,λ],F_q) ∏_ℓ ν_ℓ([X,λ],F_q). Here dim(A_g)=g(g+1)/2 and τ_T is the Tamagawa number of the algebraic torus associated with [X,λ].

paper.title: Word Measures on Symmetric Groups

global_context.setup: Fix a word w in a free group F on r generators. A w-random permutation in S_N is obtained by sampling r independent uniformly random permutations and evaluating w on them. For a class function f on S_N, let E_w[f] be its expectation under the w-measure. Consider the primitivity rank π(w), the minimal rank of a subgroup H ≤ F containing w as a non-primitive element, with crit(w) the set of such subgroups of rank π(w). For integers α_1,...,α_k, let ξ_i be the number of i-cycles in a permutation, and let ξ^α = ∏ ξ_i^{α_i}. Let E_unif denote expectation under uniform measure on S_N.
global_context.target_theorem: For every non-power w ∈ F, and for every k ∈ ℤ_{≥1} and α_1,...,α_k ∈ ℤ_{≥0}, there exists a positive integer C_α ∈ ℤ_{≥1} such that E_w[ξ^α] = E_unif[ξ^α] + C_α · |crit(w)| / N^{π(w)-1} + O(1/N^{π(w)}). Moreover, C_α = ⟨ ξ^α, ξ_1 - 1 ⟩.

However, when applied to the morphism $\eta_{\ak}^{w}\colon\Gamma_{\ak}^{w}\to X_{B}$ with $\sum i\alpha_{i}\ge2$, \eqref{eq:content of naive Thm about Phi} does not yield anything new: it only recovers earlier results from \cite{nica1994number,Linial2010}. paper.title: Maximal polarization for periodic configurations on the real line

global_context.setup: Consider 1-periodic configurations Γ on ℝ of density n, i.e., Γ = ∪_{k=1}^n (ℤ + x_k) with distinct x_k in [0,1). For α>0, define p_α(x)=∑_{γ∈Γ} e^{-π α (x-γ)^2}. The problem is to maximize min_{x∈ℝ} p_α(x) over all such configurations.
global_context.target_theorem: For n ≥ N(α) (depending only on α) and among all 1-periodic configurations Γ ⊂ ℝ of density n, i.e., Γ = ∪_{k=1}^n (ℤ + x_k), {x_1,…,x_n} ⊂ [0,1), x_k ≠ x_ℓ for k≠ℓ, the quantity min_{x∈ℝ} p_α(x) = min_{x∈ℝ} ∑_{γ∈Γ} e^{-π α (x-γ)^2}, α>0, is maximized if and only if the points are equispaced. Moreover, for n ≥ N(α) sufficiently large, min_{x∈𝕋} f_α(x) = min_{x∈𝕋} ∑_{j=1}^n θ_α(x - x_j) is maximized if and only if the points are equispaced.

This follows by a simple application of the Poisson Summation Formula and the triangle inequality. This allows for various additional tools to be used, in particular, it allows for a lossless application of the triangle inequality. We give the proof for the integers $\Z$ but the proof can easily be adjusted to scaled integers $\delta \Z$ (replace $k$ by $k/\delta$ and adjust the Poisson Summation Formula accordingly). global_context.target_theorem: Let [X,λ] be a principally polarized abelian variety over F_q with commutative endomorphism ring. Suppose that either X is ordinary or that F_q = F_p is the prime field. Then #~I([X,λ],F_q) = q^{dim(A_g)/2} τ_T ν_∞([X,λ],F_q) ∏_ℓ ν_ℓ([X,λ],F_q). The Gelfand-Graev representation is \overline{\Gamma} = c-Ind_{\overline{U}}^{\overline{G}} Ψ, and let \overline{E}_{q,n} = End_{W(k)[\overline{G}]}(\overline{\Gamma}). Let B_{q,n} be the algebra of functions on the set of conjugacy classes of maximal tori in \overline{G} that are compatible with the Curtis homomorphisms Φ_{\overline{T}}: \overline{E}_{q,n} → W(k)[\overline{T}] induced by Deligne-Lusztig restriction.

As in the previous case, all horizontal maps become isomorphisms after inverting $\ell$ and the bottom horizontal map is an isomorphism integrally.

By Proposition~\ref{prop:kernel} $K$ is contained in the subalgebra $\overline{E}_{q_{\taubar},n,1}[\Theta_{n,n}^{\pm 1}]$ of $Z_n$, and the image of this subalgebra in $R_{q_{\taubar},n}$ is saturated. It follows that the map from $K$ to $K'$ is an isomorphism: if $x$ is an element of $K'$, then for some $a$, the product $\ell^a x$ is in the image of $K$. But then $\ell^a x$ is in the image of $\overline{E}_{q_{\taubar},n,1}[\Theta_{n,n}^{\pm 1}]$, so $x$ is as well. On the other hand, the image of $K$ in $\overline{E}_{q_{\taubar},n,1}$ is saturated (as $K$ is the kernel of a map of rings that have no $\ell$-torsion), so $x$ must lie in the image of $K$.

Let $r$ be an element of $R_{q_{\taubar},n}^{\inv}$, and let $r'$ be its image in $(R_{q_{\taubar},m}^{\inv})^{\otimes j}$. There is then an element $y$ of $Z_m^{\otimes j}$ whose image under the bottom horizontal map is $r'$. Since the map $Z_n \rightarrow R_{q_{\taubar},n}^{\inv}$ is an isomorphism after inverting $\ell$, there exists $a$ such that $\ell^a y$ is in the image of $\Ind_{m,n}$.

By Theorem~\ref{thm:near saturation}, there exist $\tilde y$ in $Z_n$ and $x$ in $\overline{E}_{q_{\taubar},n,1}[\Theta_{n,n}^{\pm 1}]$ such that $\Ind_{m,n}(x) = \ell^b(\Ind_{m,n}(\tilde y) - y)$. Let $s$ be the image of $\tilde y$ in $R_{q_{\taubar},n}^{\inv}$. The image of $\ell^b(s-r)$ in $(R_{q_{\taubar},m}^{\inv})^{\otimes j}$ coincides with the image of $\Ind_{m,n}(x)$. global_context.setup: Let I = (-ρ, ρ) with 0 < ρ ≤ ∞. For integers n ≥ 1 and 0 ≤ k ≤ n, let 𝒮_n^{(k)}(I) be the set of n×n symmetric matrices with entries in I having exactly k negative eigenvalues (counted with multiplicity). Let 𝒮^{(k)}(I) = ∪_{n≥k} 𝒮_n^{(k)}(I). Define the closure \overline{𝒮_n^{(k)}}(I) = ∪_{j=0}^k 𝒮_n^{(j)}(I).
global_context.target_theorem: Let I = (-ρ, ρ), where 0 < ρ ≤ ∞, and let k and l be positive integers. Given f : I → ℝ, the following are equivalent: (1) f[-] sends \overline{𝒮_n^{(k)}}(I) to \overline{𝒮_n^{(l)}} for all n ≥ k; (2) f[-] sends 𝒮_n^{(k)}(I) to \overline{𝒮_n^{(l)}} for all n ≥ k; (3) either f is constant, or l ≥ k and f is linear: f(x) = f(0) + c x with c > 0 and f(0) ≥ 0 if l = k. If k ≥ 1 and l = 0, then f[-] sends \overline{𝒮_n^{(k)}}(I) to 𝒮_n^{(0)} for all n ≥ k iff f is constant nonnegative. If k = 0 and l ≥ 1, then f[-] sends 𝒮_n^{(0)}(I) to \overline{𝒮_n^{(l)}} for all n ≥ 1 iff f(x) = ∑_{n=0}^∞ c_n x^n on I with all c_n ≥ 0 for n ≥ 1.

paper.title: Infinitesimal structure of the pluricanonical double ramification locus

global_context.setup: Let $g,n$ be nonnegative integers, $k \geq 1$ an integer, and $\mathbf{m} = (m_1,\ldots,m_n) \in \mathbb{Z}^n$ with $\sum m_i = k(2g-2)$. Let $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}$ be the moduli space of stable $n$-pointed curves of genus $g$. The double ramification cycle $\overline{\mathrm{DRC}}$ is defined via extending the Abel–Jacobi section $\sigma: \mathcal{M}_{g,n} \to \mathcal{J}$, $\sigma(C,p_i) = \omega_C^{\otimes k}(-\sum m_i p_i)$, to a blow-up $\mathcal{M}^\lozenge$ of $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}$, pulling back the zero section, and pushing forward. The weighted fundamental class $H_{g,\mathbf{m}}^k$ is defined as the weighted fundamental class of the moduli space $\widetilde{\mathcal{H}}_g^k(\mathbf{m})$ of twisted $k$-differentials, consisting of stable curves admitting a twist $I$ on the dual graph such that on the partial normalization there exists an isomorphism $\omega_{C_I}^{\otimes k} \cong \mathcal{O}_{C_I}\left(\sum m_i p_i + \sum (I(h)-k) q_h + (I(h')-k) q_{h'}\right)$.

Moreover, we have $\overline{\mathrm{DRC}} = H_{g,\mathbf{m}}^k$ in the Chow group $A^g(\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n})$, and even as cycles on $\widetilde{\mathcal{H}}_g^k(\mathbf{m})$.

paper.title: Complete moduli of cubic threefolds and their intermediate Jacobians

global_context.setup: Let $\mathcal{M}$ be the moduli space of smooth cubic threefolds in $\mathbb{P}^4$, and $\mathcal{A}_5$ the moduli space of principally polarized abelian fivefolds. The intermediate Jacobian map $IJ:\mathcal{M}\to\mathcal{A}_5$ associates to a cubic threefold its intermediate Jacobian. There is a GIT compactification $\overline{\mathcal{M}}$ of $\mathcal{M}$, and its wonderful blow-up $\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}$. On the other hand, $\mathcal{A}_5$ has the second Voronoi toroidal compactification $\overline{\mathcal{A}}_5^V$.
global_context.target_theorem: The map $IJ: \calM\to\calA_5$ sending a smooth cubic threefold to its intermediate Jacobian extends to a morphism $\widetilde{IJ}^{V}:\tM\to\Vor[5]$ from the wonderful blow-up of the GIT compactification of the moduli space of cubic threefolds to the second Voronoi toroidal compactification of the moduli space of principally polarized abelian fivefolds.

paper.title: Permanence properties of $F$-injectivity

global_context.setup: Noetherian rings of prime characteristic p. F-injectivity for a local ring (R, m) means the Frobenius map induces injective maps on all local cohomology modules H^i_m(R). A ring is F-injective if its localizations at all maximal ideals are F-injective. Consider flat and faithfully flat homomorphisms of Noetherian rings, and fibers that are Cohen-Macaulay and geometrically F-injective.
global_context.target_theorem: For arbitrary Noetherian rings of prime characteristic: (1) F-injectivity localizes. (2) F-injectivity descends under faithfully flat homomorphisms. (3) F-injectivity ascends under flat homomorphisms with Cohen–Macaulay and geometrically F-injective fibers. Consequently, the F-injective locus is open on most rings arising in arithmetic and geometry.

local_context: \par As an application of our main theorem, we prove that $F$-injectivity is an open condition for most rings that arise in geometric applications, in particular resolving (\ref{property:openness}) and answering a question of the second author \cite[Rem.\ 3.6]{Mur}.

\begin{customthm}{\ref{thm:finjlocusopen}} Let $R$ be a ring essentially of finite type over a Noetherian local ring $(A,\fm)$ of prime characteristic $p > 0$, and suppose that $A$ has Cohen--Macaulay and geometrically $F$-injective formal fibers. Then, the $F$-injective locus is open in $\Spec(R)$. \end{customthm}

\noindent The condition on formal fibers holds if $A$ is excellent. At the same time, we also observe that the $F$-injective locus need not be open if $R$ is merely locally excellent (see Example \ref{ex:finjnotopen}).

\medskip \par Finally, we give a geometric application of our results. Bombieri \cite[p.\ 209]{Bom73} and Andreotti--Holm \cite[p.\ 91]{AH77} asked whether the image of a smooth projective variety of dimension $r$ under a generic projection to $\PP^{r+1}_k$ is weakly normal. Greco--Traverso \cite[Thm.\ 3.7]{GT80} proved that this is indeed the case over the complex numbers, and the case in arbitrary characteristic follows from work of Roberts--Zaare-Nahandi \citeleft\citen{ZN84}\citemid Thm.\ 3.2\citepunct\citen{RZN84}\citemid Thm.\ 1.1\citeright\ and Cumino--Manaresi \cite[Thm.\ 3.8]{CM81} (see \cite[Obs.\ 2.4$(v)$]{CGM89}). As an application of our results, we show that generic projection hypersurfaces associated to suitably embedded smooth varieties of low dimension are in fact $F$-pure (hence also $F$-injective) in positive characteristic, which is a stronger condition than being weakly normal \cite[Thm.\ 7.3]{SZ13}.

\begin{customthm}{\ref{thm:dohertyanalogue}} Let $Y \subseteq \PP^n_k$ be a smooth projective variety of dimension $r \le 5$ over an algebraically closed field $k$ of characteristic $p > r$, such that $Y$ is embedded via the $d$-uple embedding with $d \ge 3r$. If $\pi\colon Y \to \PP^{r+1}_k$ is a generic projection and $X = \pi(Y)$, then $X$ is $F$-pure, and hence $F$-injective. \end{customthm}

global_context.setup: Let k be an algebraically closed field of characteristic 0. Let X be a smooth affine surface over k, and f: X -> X a finite surjective endomorphism. Assume the étale fundamental group π_1^et(X) is infinite. For AZO, assume the transcendence degree of k over Q is finite; for KSC, assume k = Qbar.
global_context.target_theorem: Let X be a smooth affine surface and f: X -> X a finite surjective endomorphism. Suppose the étale fundamental group π_1^et(X) is infinite. Then: (1) AZO and hence ZDO hold for (X,f). (2) Suppose k = Qbar, deg(f) ≥ 2 and X is not isomorphic to A^1 × G_m. Then KSC holds for (X,f). (3) Suppose that κ(X) = -∞. Then f descends along an A^1-fibration of X, hence KSC holds for (X,f) when deg(f)=1 and k = Qbar.

paper.title: Skeletons of Prym varieties and Brill–Noethertheory

global_context.setup: Let π: \widetilde{X} → X be an unramified double cover of a smooth projective curve over a non-Archimedean field with nontrivial valuation. Let Γ be the minimal skeleton of the Berkovich analytification of X, and let \widetilde{Γ} → Γ be the induced tropical double cover. The Prym variety Pr(X,π) is a principally polarized abelian variety, and its non-Archimedean skeleton Σ(Pr(X,π)) is a principally polarized tropical abelian variety. The tropical Prym variety Pr(Γ) is defined as the tropical Jacobian of \widetilde{Γ} with the pushforward of the principal polarization.
global_context.target_theorem: We show that the non-Archimedean skeleton of the Prym variety associated to an unramified double cover of an algebraic curve is naturally isomorphic (as a principally polarized tropical abelian variety) to the tropical Prym variety of the associated tropical double cover.

paper.title: p-adic Borel extension for local Shimura varieties

global_context.setup: Local Shimura data (G, [b], {μ}) where G is a reductive group over Q_p, [b] a σ-conjugacy class, {μ} a conjugacy class of geometric cocharacters, K ⊂ G(Q_p) compact open. The local Shimura variety CM_{G,b,μ,K} is defined over the completion of the maximal unramified extension of the reflex field E. Morphisms are rigid analytic maps from products of punctured and unpunctured rigid analytic closed unit disks over a finite extension F of that field.
global_context.target_theorem: Every rigid analytic morphism a^×: BB^{×s} × BB^t → CM_{G,b,μ,K} over Sp(\br E) extends uniquely to a: BB^{s+t} → CM_{G,b,μ,K} over Sp(\br E).

\newcommand{\und}{\underline} \newcommand{\sk}{\vskip0.02in} \begin{document} \title[$p$-adic Borel extension for local Shimura varieties]{$p$-adic Borel extension for local Shimura varieties}
\author{Abhishek Oswal} \address{Mathematisches Institut, Albert-Ludwigs-Universität Freiburg, Freiburg, Germany} \email{abhishek.oswal@math.uni-freiburg.de}
\author{Georgios Pappas}\address{Department of Mathematics, Michigan State University, E. Lansing, MI 48824, USA} \email{pappasg@msu.edu}

\begin{abstract} We show that the moduli spaces of Scholze's $p$-adic shtukas with framing satisfy a $p$-adic rigid analytic version of Borel's extension theorem. In particular, this holds for local Shimura varieties, for all local Shimura data $(G,[b],\{\mu\})$, even for exceptional groups $G$, and extends work of Oswal-Shankar-Zhu-Patel who proved a $p$-adic Borel extension property for Rapoport-Zink spaces. As a corollary, we deduce that all these spaces satisfy a $p$-adic rigid analytic version of Brody hyperbolicity. \end{abstract}

global_context.target_theorem: Fix κ∈ℝ and a horizontal geodesic Γ⊂𝕄²(κ)×{0}. There exists a family Σ_{λ,H}^*, parametrized by λ≥0 and H>0 such that 4H²+κ>0, of complete H-surfaces in 𝕄²(κ)×ℝ, invariant under a discrete group of translations along Γ with respect to which they are cylindrically bounded. Moreover: (i) Σ_{0,H}^* is the invariant H-cylinder (H-torus if κ>0); (ii) for 0<λ<π/2, Σ_{λ,H}^* is the unduloid from [MT]; (iii) Σ_{π/2,H}^* is a stack of tangent rotationally invariant H-spheres; (iv) for λ>π/2, Σ_{λ,H}^* is a nodoid-type surface.

global_context.setup: We consider strongly regular vertex operator algebras (VOAs) V of rank 3, i.e., with exactly three simple modules. The q-characters f_i(τ) = Tr_{M_i} q^{L(0)-c/24} form the character vector F(τ). By Zhu's Theorem, the span ch_V is a right Γ-module for Γ = SL_2(ℤ), and F(τ) is a weight zero vector-valued modular form with representation ρ. We assume that the character vector satisfies a monic modular linear differential equation (MLDE) of order 3: (D^3 + a E_4 D + b E_6) u = 0, with irreducible monodromy.
global_context.target_theorem: \begin{thm}[Rank $3$ Mathur-Mukhi-Sen] \label{thmmain} Let $V$ be a strongly regular VOA with exactly three simple modules. Suppose that the $q$-characters of the simple $V$-modules furnish a fundamental system of solutions for an MLDE of order $3$ that is (i) monic, and (ii) has irreducible monodromy. Then one of the following holds: \begin{enumerate} \item[(a)]$V$ is isomorphic to one of the following: \begin{eqnarray*} &&B_{\ell,1} \quad\quad (\ell \geq 2),\\ &&A_{1,2},\\ &&\Vir(c_{3, 4}),~ \Vir(c_{2,7}). \end{eqnarray*} \item[(b)] $V$ lies in the $U$-series (cf. Remark \ref{r:useries}). \end{enumerate} (Here, and below, $\mathcal{G}_{\ell, k}$ denotes an affine algebra of type $\mathcal{G}$, rank $\ell$, and level $k$; $\Vir(c)$ is a Virasoro VOA of central charge $c$.) \end{thm}

For a point $P \in D$, $\mathcal{M}$ has good formal decomposition at $P$ if, after possibly ramification, the formal completion of $\mathcal{M}$ at $P$ splits as a direct sum of well-understood systems. The set $\Good(\mathcal{M}) \subset D$ is the set of points where $\mathcal{M}$ has good formal decomposition. We consider the restrictions of $\Sol \mathcal{M}$ and $\Sol \End(\mathcal{M})$ to $D$, and the condition that these are local systems on $D$.

paper.title: Permanence properties of $F$-injectivity

global_context.setup: Noetherian rings of prime characteristic p. F-injectivity for a local ring (R, m) means the Frobenius map induces injective maps on all local cohomology modules H^i_m(R). A ring is F-injective if its localizations at all maximal ideals are F-injective. Consider flat and faithfully flat homomorphisms of Noetherian rings, and fibers that are Cohen-Macaulay and geometrically F-injective.
global_context.target_theorem: For arbitrary Noetherian rings of prime characteristic: (1) F-injectivity localizes. (2) F-injectivity descends under faithfully flat homomorphisms. (3) F-injectivity ascends under flat homomorphisms with Cohen–Macaulay and geometrically F-injective fibers. Consequently, the F-injective locus is open on most rings arising in arithmetic and geometry.

local_context: \par As an application of our main theorem, we prove that $F$-injectivity is an open condition for most rings that arise in geometric applications, in particular resolving (\ref{property:openness}) and answering a question of the second author \cite[Rem.\ 3.6]{Mur}.

\begin{customthm}{\ref{thm:finjlocusopen}} Let $R$ be a ring essentially of finite type over a Noetherian local ring $(A,\fm)$ of prime characteristic $p > 0$, and suppose that $A$ has Cohen--Macaulay and geometrically $F$-injective formal fibers. Then, the $F$-injective locus is open in $\Spec(R)$. \end{customthm}

\noindent The condition on formal fibers holds if $A$ is excellent. At the same time, we also observe that the $F$-injective locus need not be open if $R$ is merely locally excellent (see Example \ref{ex:finjnotopen}).

\medskip \par Finally, we give a geometric application of our results. Bombieri \cite[p.\ 209]{Bom73} and Andreotti--Holm \cite[p.\ 91]{AH77} asked whether the image of a smooth projective variety of dimension $r$ under a generic projection to $\PP^{r+1}_k$ is weakly normal. Greco--Traverso \cite[Thm.\ 3.7]{GT80} proved that this is indeed the case over the complex numbers, and the case in arbitrary characteristic follows from work of Roberts--Zaare-Nahandi \citeleft\citen{ZN84}\citemid Thm.\ 3.2\citepunct\citen{RZN84}\citemid Thm.\ 1.1\citeright\ and Cumino--Manaresi \cite[Thm.\ 3.8]{CM81} (see \cite[Obs.\ 2.4$(v)$]{CGM89}). As an application of our results, we show that generic projection hypersurfaces associated to suitably embedded smooth varieties of low dimension are in fact $F$-pure (hence also $F$-injective) in positive characteristic, which is a stronger condition than being weakly normal \cite[Thm.\ 7.3]{SZ13}.

global_context.setup: Let g,n be nonnegative integers with 2g+n>2. The moduli space Q_{g,n} of meromorphic quadratic differentials with at most n simple poles and no other poles on genus g curves carries the Masur–Veech volume. For a stable graph Γ in G_{g,n}, define polynomial P_Γ as in (eq:P:Gamma) using polynomials N_{g_v,n_v} which are expressed via intersection numbers on moduli spaces M_{g_v,n_v}.
global_context.target_theorem: The Masur–Veech volume Vol Q_{g,n} = Vol QuadStrat(1^{4g-4+n}, -1^n) equals ∑_{Γ∈G_{g,n}} Vol(Γ) = ∑_{Γ∈G_{g,n}} ∑_{H∈N^{E(Γ)}} Vol(Γ,H), where Vol(Γ)=Z(P_Γ) and Vol(Γ,H)=Y(H)(P_Γ) for certain linear operators Z and Y.

local_context: Recall that we denote by $\cST(\QuadStrat(1^{4g-4+n}, -1^n), N)$ the set of square-tiled surfaces in the principal stratum $\QuadStrat(1^{4g-4+n}, -1^n)$ tiled with at most $N$ squares. Define now the quantity: $$ \cD\cST(\QuadStrat(1^{4g-4+n}, -1^n), N) := \sum_{S \in \cST(\QuadStrat(1^{4g-4+n}, -1^n), N)} M(S)\,. $$

The latter quantity has the following geometric interpretation. Consider all square-tiled surfaces obtained from square-tiled surfaces as above by cutting exactly one cylinder $\cyl_i$ along the closed horizontal trajectory at the level $h$, where $0\le h< h_i$ and $h$ is integer in the case of Abelian differentials and half-integer in the case of quadratic differentials. In other words, we do not chop the squares along the cut. The above quantity enumerates bordered square-tiled surfaces obtained in this way. Indeed, we lose the twist parameter $w_i$ (correspondingly $2w_i$) along the cylinder which is now cut open, but we gain the new height parameter $h_i$ (correspondingly $2h_i$) responsible for the level of the cut.
As a corollary of the above theorem, D.~Chen and A.~Eskin proved the following result.

\begin{NNTheorem}[D.~Chen, A.~Eskin, Appendix A in~\cite{Chen}] The area Siegel--Veech constant $\carea(\cQ_{g,n})=\carea(\QuadStrat(1^{4g-4+n},-1^n))$ has the following value: \begin{equation} \label{eq:SVconstant:for:stratum} \carea(\QuadStrat(1^{4g-4+n},-1^n))= \cfrac{3}{\pi^2}\cdot \lim_{N\to\infty} \frac{\cD\cST(\QuadStrat(1^{4g-4+n}, -1^n), 2N)} {\card(\cST(\QuadStrat(1^{4g-4+n}, -1^n), 2N))}\,. \end{equation} \end{NNTheorem} Formally speaking, the original Theorem is proved only for the components of the strata of Abelian differentials. However, all the arguments are applicable to any arithmetic $\GL$-invariant submanifold, in particular to the loci induced from strata of quadratic differentials by the ramified double covering construction.

An alternative way to derive~\eqref{eq:SVconstant:for:stratum} is to use the following more elaborate technique. Neglecting exceptional orbits of square-tiled surfaces in $\QuadStrat(1^{4g-4+n},-1^n)$ containing negligibly small number of square-tiled surfaces, we can arrange other orbits in a sequence of affine invariant manifolds for which the natural $\SL$-invariant measures supported on the orbits converge to the

global_context.setup: Saturated fusion systems over finite p-groups, components, realizable fusion systems, normal fusion subsystems. For a prime p, a saturated fusion system F over a finite p-group S is realized by a finite group G if S is a Sylow p-subgroup of G and morphisms in F are induced by conjugation in G. paper.title: Horizontal Delaunay surfaces with constant mean curvature in $\mathbb{S}^2 \times \mathbb{R}$ and $\mathbb{H}^2 \times \mathbb{R}$

global_context.setup: We work in the product spaces M^2(κ)×R, where M^2(κ) is the simply connected surface of constant curvature κ. Consider a horizontal geodesic Γ in M^2(κ)×{0} and constant mean curvature H > 0 such that 4H^2+κ > 0. The surfaces of interest are invariant under discrete translations along Γ and cylindrically bounded with respect to Γ.
global_context.target_theorem: Fix κ∈R and a horizontal geodesic Γ⊂M^2(κ)×{0}. There exists a family Σ_{λ,H}^*, parametrized by λ≥0 and H>0 such that 4H^2+κ>0, of complete H-surfaces in M^2(κ)×R, invariant under a discrete group of translations along Γ with respect to which they are cylindrically bounded. They are symmetric about M^2(κ)×{0} and Γ×R. Moreover: (i) Σ_{0,H}^* is the invariant H-cylinder (H-torus if κ>0); (ii) Σ_{λ,H}^* is the unduloid-type surface from [MT] if 0<λ<π/2; (iii) Σ_{π/2,H}^* is a stack of tangent rotationally invariant H-spheres; (iv) Σ_{λ,H}^* is a nodoid-type surface if λ>π/2.

paper.title: Combining rational maps and Kleinian groups via orbit equivalence

global_context.setup: The paper develops a framework for mating Kleinian groups with rational maps. It considers finitely generated non-elementary Kleinian groups in the closure of a Bers slice of a Fuchsian lattice, and Bers rational maps (rational maps with a simply connected completely invariant Fatou component). The mating aims to combine the dynamics via a dynamically natural homeomorphism between the limit set and the Julia set.
global_context.target_theorem: The only torsion-free Fuchsian groups that can be mated with rational maps in this framework are punctured sphere groups.

local_context: Omega(\Gamma)$ is mapped homeomorphically onto the boundary of a principal component by some positive iterate of $A_{\Gamma, \mathrm{BS}}$. It follows that the boundary of each strictly pre-periodic bounded Fatou component of $P_\Gamma$ is mapped homeomorphically onto the boundary of a periodic Fatou component by some iterate of $P_\Gamma$. This shows that no strictly pre-periodic bounded Fatou component of $P_\Gamma$ contains a critical point. Hence, every finite critical point of the postcritically finite polynomial $P_\Gamma$ lies in a periodic bounded Fatou component.

2) Let $\mathcal{U}$ be an equatorial Fatou component of $P_\Gamma$. Then $\pmb{\Phi}(\partial\mathcal{U})$ is the boundary of an equatorial principal component of $\Omega(\Gamma)$, and hence $A_{\Gamma, \mathrm{BS}}:\pmb{\Phi}(\partial\mathcal{U})\to\pmb{\Phi}(\partial\mathcal{U})$ is a covering of some degree $k\geq 2$ (by part (1) of Theorem~\ref{first_return_conf_model_thm}). Therefore, $P_\Gamma:\partial\mathcal{U}\to\partial\mathcal{U}$ is a covering map of degree $k\geq 2$, so $P_\Gamma:\mathcal{U}\to\mathcal{U}$ is a proper branched covering of degree $k$. Choose (the homeomorphic extension of) a Riemann uniformization $\phi_{\mathcal{U}}:\overline{\D}\to\overline{\mathcal{U}}$ that sends $0$ to a critical point of $P_\Gamma$. Then, $\phi_{\mathcal{U}}^{-1}\circ P_\Gamma\circ \phi_{\mathcal{U}}$ is a degree $k$ proper branched covering of $\D$, and hence a Blaschke product $B$ of degree $k$. Moreover, postcritical finiteness of $P_\Gamma$ implies postcritical finiteness of $B$. Hence, possibly after pre-composing $\phi_{\mathcal{U}}$ with an element of $\mathrm{Aut}(\D)$, we can assume that the postcritically finite degree $k$ Blaschke product $B$ is of the form $z\mapsto z^k$ (for instance, see \cite[Lemma~4.17]{milnor-hyp-comp}).

3) Let $\mathcal{U}^+=\mathcal{U}$ be a polar Fatou component of $P_\Gamma$. Then $\pmb{\Phi}(\partial\mathcal{U}^+)$ is the boundary of a polar principal component $U^+$ of $\Omega(\Gamma)$. Suppose that $\partial U^-=A_{\Gamma, \mathrm{BS}}(\partial U^+)$; equivalently, $U^-$ is the image polar principal component under $\widehat{A}_{\Gamma, \mathrm{BS}}$. By part (2) of Theorem~\ref{first_return_conf_model_thm}, the maps $A_{\Gamma, \mathrm{BS}}:\partial U^\pm\to\partial U^\mp$ are coverings of (a common) degree $k\geq 2$.

Let $\mathcal{U}^-$ be the image of $\mathcal{U}^+$ under $P_\Gamma$, so $\pmb{\Phi}(\partial\mathcal{U}^-)=\partial U^-$. It now follows from the previous paragraph that $P_\Gamma:\partial \mathcal{U}^\pm\to\partial \mathcal{U}^\mp$ are coverings of (a common) degree $k\geq 2$. Hence, $P_\Gamma:\mathcal{U}^\pm\to\mathcal{U}^\mp$ are proper branched coverings of degree $k$. Choose (homeomorphic extensions of) Riemann uniformizations $\phi_\pm:\overline{\D}\to\overline{\mathcal{U}^\pm}$ that send $0$ to critical points of $P_\Gamma$ (in $\mathcal{U}^\pm$). Then, the maps $\phi_{\mp}^{-1}\circ P_\Gamma\vert_{\mathcal{U}^\pm}\circ \phi_{\pm}$ are degree $k$ proper branched coverings of $\D$, and hence are Blaschke products of degree $k$ with a critical point at the origin. We call these Blaschke products $B_+, B_-$ (respectively). global_context.setup: Let E be an elliptic curve over Q, p an odd prime of good reduction. Let X be the complement of the origin in the minimal regular model of E. For each bad prime q, define a set W_q as per reduction type (see Table 1). Define Coleman integrals Log(z) = ∫_b^z ω and D_2(z) = ∫_b^z ωη, where ω = dx/(2y+a_1x+a_3), η = xω, and b is the integral tangent vector at O dual to ω.
global_context.target_theorem: Suppose that E has rank 0 and that the p-primary part of the Tate–Shafarevich group is finite. (2) Otherwise, X(Z_p)_2 = ∪_{w∈W} φ(w), where φ(w) = {z ∈ X(Z_p) : Log(z)=0, 2D_2(z)+||w||=0}.

paper.title: On the Gross-Prasad conjecture with its refinement for $\left(\mathrm{SO}\left(5\right),\mathrm{SO}\left(2\right)\right)$ and the generalized Böcherer conjecture

global_context.setup: Let F be a number field with adele ring A, E a quadratic extension. Let G_D = SO(5) be the special orthogonal group of a 5-dimensional quadratic space over F (possibly coming from a quaternion algebra D). Let π be an irreducible cuspidal automorphic representation of G_D(A) with trivial central character. Let Λ be a character of A_E^x/E^x such that Λ|_A^x = 1. The (E,Λ)-Bessel period of π is an integral over a torus embedding of SO(2) ≅ E^1. Assume π has such a Bessel period.
global_context.target_theorem: Suppose π has the (E,Λ)-Bessel period and there exists a finite place w such that π_w and its local theta lift to GSO_{4,2}(F_w) are generic. Then there exists a finite set S0 of places of F containing all archimedean places such that for any finite set S containing S0, the partial L-function L^S(1/2, π × AI(Λ)) is non-zero.

paper.title: Quenched universality for deformed Wigner matrices

global_context.setup: Wigner matrices H are N×N Hermitian random matrices with independent identically distributed entries (up to symmetry) h_ab, normalized so that eigenvalues follow the semicircular distribution ρ_sc. The bulk regime consists of energies E where ρ_sc(E) ≥ c > 0. For an interval I in the bulk with length at least N^{-1+ξ}, we consider the empirical distribution of rescaled eigenvalue gaps Nρ_sc(λ_i)(λ_{i+1}−λ_i) for eigenvalues λ_i ∈ I.
global_context.target_theorem: Let H be a Wigner matrix and I ⊂ (-2+ε, 2-ε) be an interval in the bulk of H of length |I| ≥ N^{-1+ξ} for some ε, ξ > 0. Then there exist small κ, α > 0 and an event Ω_I in the probability space of H with Prob_H(Ω_I^c) ≤ N^{-κ}, such that for all H ∈ Ω_I, sup_{y≥0} | (1/(N∫_I ρ_sc)) #{ i : N ρ_sc(λ_i)(λ_{i+1}−λ_i) ≤ y, λ_i ∈ I } − ∫_0^y p_β(t) dt | = O(N^{-α}), where p_β is the universal Wigner-Dyson-Mehta gap density.

paper.title: Fine mesh limit of the VRJP in dimension one and Bass-Burdzy flow

global_context.setup: The paper studies a fine mesh limit of the Vertex Reinforced Jump Process (VRJP) on the one-dimensional lattice 2^{-n}Z. For each n, define X^{(n)}_t as the VRJP starting at 0 with uniform conductances C=2^{2n-1} and initial local times phi_{i2^{-n}} = 2^{-n} L_0(i2^{-n}), where L_0: R -> (0,∞) is continuous and satisfies ∫_0^∞ L_0(x)^{-2} dx = ∫_{-∞}^0 L_0(x)^{-2} dx = ∞. The limit is a Linearly Reinforced Motion (LRM) on R, constructed via the Bass-Burdzy equation dY_u/du = -1 if Y_u > B_u, 1 if Y_u < B_u, with B a Brownian motion, and a time change.
global_context.target_theorem: The VRJP process jointly with its occupation profiles (X^{(n)}_t, L^{(n)}_t(x))_{x∈2^{-n}Z, 0≤t≤t^{(n)}_{max}} converge in law as n→∞ to a Linearly Reinforced Motion started from 0 and its occupation profiles (X_t, L_t(x))_{x∈R, t≥0}. The convergence is uniform on compact subsets, and t^{(n)}_{max} converges in probability to +∞.

paper.title: On questions of uniqueness for the vacant set of Wiener sausages and Brownian interlacements

global_context.setup: Let d ≥ 3. Brownian interlacements at intensity α > 0 is a Poisson point process on the space of doubly-infinite Brownian paths modulo time shift, with intensity measure αμ, where μ is a measure constructed from Brownian motion and hitting distributions. The Brownian interlacements set BI^α is the union of the Wiener sausages of radius a > 0 (fixed) of the paths in the process. The vacant set V^α = ℝ^d \ BI^α.
global_context.target_theorem: For any α > 0, the vacant set V^α contains at most one infinite connected component almost surely.

paper.title: Lack of null-controllability for the fractional heat equation and related equations

global_context.setup: Consider the fractional heat equation (∂t + (-Δ)^{α/2}) f = 1_ω u on [0,T] × Ω, with Ω = R or T, ω an open subset of Ω, and α ≥ 0. Null-controllability on ω in time T means for every f0 in L^2(Ω), there exists u in L^2([0,T]×ω) such that the solution with f(0)=f0 satisfies f(T)=0.
global_context.target_theorem: Let 0 ≤ α < 1 and Ω = R or T. Let ω be a strict open subset of Ω. The fractional heat equation is not null-controllable on ω in any time T > 0.

See the rightmost subfigure in Figure~\ref{fig:trifurcation} for the level set decomposition of the difference function $\hat g$ corresponding to a sample from $T$. We claim that $\Omega(\Pi_m,f_1)\times\Omega(\Pi_m,f_2)$ intersects $T$ whenever $(f_1,f_2)\in I(n)\cap L(m)$. Fix such a pair $(f_1,f_2)$ and assume, without loss of generality, that $f_1(\mathbf 0)=f_2(\mathbf 0)=0$. Assert first that there exists a height function $f''$ which equals $f_1$ on $\Pi_n$ and which equals $f_2$ on $\Pi_m^\complement$, and which at any vertex $\x$ takes values between $f_1(\x)$ and $f_2(\x)$. The following construction works: write first $f'$ for a height function which extends $f_1|_{\Pi_n}f_2|_{\Pi_m^\complement}$ to $X^d$, then define $f''$ by \[f'':=(f'\vee(f_1\wedge f_2) ) \wedge (f_1 \vee f_2).\] This proves the assertion. Now $f''\in\Omega(\Pi_m,f_2)$ as $f''$ equals $f_2$ on $\Pi_m^\complement$. Moreover, as at each vertex the value of $f''$ is in between the values of $f_1$ and $f_2$, we have $ \{f_1=f_2\}\subset\{f_1=f''\} \subset X^d$, and we also know that $\Pi_n\subset\{f_1=f''\} $. This implies that $(f_1,f'')\in T$, since the three connected components of $\{f_1=f_2\}$ are contained in a single connected component of $\{f_1=f''\}$, and because $\{f_1=f_2\}\smallsetminus\Pi_m=\{f_1=f''\}\smallsetminus\Pi_m$, that is, removing $\Pi_m$ disconnects these three components again.

paper.title: Approximation Limitations of Pure Dynamic Programming

global_context.setup: A combinatorial optimization minimization problem f_n is given by a family F ⊆ 2^{[n]} of feasible solutions. For nonnegative weights x ∈ ℝ_+^n, f_n(x) = min_{S ∈ F} ∑_{i∈ S} x_i. A tropical (min,+) circuit is a circuit with gates computing min or + operations. It r-approximates f_n if for all x, f_n(x) ≤ C(x) ≤ r·f_n(x). The boolean version of f_n is the monotone boolean function b_f: {0,1}^n → {0,1} where b_f(A)=1 iff ∃ S ∈ F with S ⊆ A.
global_context.target_theorem: If the boolean version of a minimization problem f_n requires monotone boolean (∨,∧) circuits of size > t, then no tropical (min,+) circuit of size ≤ t can approximate f_n within any finite factor r = r(n) ≥ 1.

local_context: \begin{result}[Boolean bound for $(\min,+)$ circuits; \cref{thm:bool}] \label{res:bool} If the boolean version of a minimization problem $f_n$ requires monotone boolean $(\lor,\land)$ circuits of size $>t$, then no tropical $(\min,+)$ circuit of size $\leq t$ can approximate $f_n$ within any finite factor $r=r(n)\geq 1$. \end{result}

That is, if a tropical $(\min,+)$ circuit has fewer than $t$ gates, then regardless of how large approximation factor $r$ we will allow, there will be an input weighting on which the circuit makes an error: the computed value on this input will be either strictly smaller or more than $r$ times larger than the optimal value.
Together with known lower bounds for monotone boolean circuits, \cref{res:bool} yields the same lower bounds for tropical $(\min,+)$ circuits approximating the corresponding minimization problems.

paper.title: CLAP: A New Algorithm for Promise CSPs

global_context.setup: Promise Constraint Satisfaction Problems (PCSPs) generalize CSPs. A PCSP is specified by two relational structures A and B with a homomorphism from A to B. The problem PCSP(A,B) asks: given an input structure I that maps homomorphically to A, decide if it maps homomorphically to B (promise: if I -> A then I -> B). The CLAP algorithm combines the Constraint Basic LP relaxation (BLP) and the Affine IP relaxation (AIP). Polymorphisms are operations that preserve the relations; they form a minion crucial for the algebraic approach.
global_context.target_theorem: \begin{thm}\label{thm:main1} Let $(\A,\B)$ be a PCSP template. Then, $\CLAP$ solves $\PCSP(\A,\B)$ if and only if there is a minion homomorphism from $\ourMinion$ to $\Pol(\A,\B)$. \end{thm}

local_context: \begin{rem} Consider the minion $\Mblpaip$ from~\cite{bgwz20} (cf.~Appendix~\ref{subsec:blp-aip}). A direct consequence of Example~\ref{ex:new2}, Theorem~\ref{thm:main1}, and~\cite[Lemma 5.4]{bgwz20} is that there is no minion homomorphism from $\Mblpaip$ to $\ourMinion$. On the other hand, the function \begin{align*} \vartheta:\ourMinion&\rightarrow\Mblpaip\\ (M,\bmu)&\mapsto (M\be_1,\bmu) \end{align*} is readily seen to be a minion homomorphism. It follows that $\CLAP$ solves any PCSP template solved by $\BLP+\AIP$ (as is also clear from the description of the two algorithms). \end{rem}

\begin{rem} Similar to~\cite{bgwz20}, the assumption in Theorem~\ref{thm:Hsymm} can be weakened as follows: Instead of requiring $H$-symmetric polymorphisms of arbitrarily large arity, it turns out to be enough requiring $H$-block-symmetric polymorphisms of arbitrarily large width, where the definition of an $H$-block-symmetric operation mirrors that of a block-symmetric operation in~\cite{bgwz20}. The proof of this possibly stronger result is very similar to that of Theorem~\ref{thm:Hsymm}. For completeness, we include it in Appendix~\ref{sec:block_sym_pol}. We point out that we do not know whether the condition in Theorem~\ref{thm:Hsymm} (or the possibly weaker condition based on $H$-block-symmetric polymorphisms) is necessary for tractability via CLAP, but we suspect it is not. \end{rem}

\begin{rem} A possibly stronger version of the $\CLAP$ algorithm consists in running $\BLP+\AIP$ (instead of just $\BLP$) at each iteration in the \textbf{for} loop in lines $5$--$9$ of Algorithm~\ref{alg:clap}, and then removing the additional \textbf{for} loop in lines $14$--$18$. This algorithm can be called C($\BLP+\AIP$). An analysis entirely analogous to the one presented in this paper shows that the power of C($\BLP+\AIP$) is captured by the minion $\tilde{\ourMinion}$ defined like $\ourMinion$ with the following difference: The $L$-ary elements of $\tilde{\ourMinion}$ are pairs $(M,N)$, where $M$ is as in $\ourMinion$ while $N$ is an integer matrix of the same size as $M$ taking the role of $\bmu$ (in particular, $N$ satisfies the \enquote{refinement condition} $\supp(N\be_i)\subseteq\supp(M\be_i)$ $\forall i\in\N$, analogous to $(c_4)$ in Definition~\ref{def:minion}). A possible direction for future research is to investigate whether the richer structure of $\tilde{\ourMinion}$ can be exploited to obtain a stronger version of

paper.title: Multiplicative SHE limit of random walks in space-time random environments

global_context.setup: We consider a 1D random walk in a space-time random environment with iid [0,1]-valued transition probabilities ω_{t,x} having mean 1/2 and variance σ² ∈ [0,1/4). For N ≥ 1 and t ∈ N^{-1}ℤ_{≥0}, define the quenched density field U_N(t,·) as the random measure: U_N(t,·) = ∑_{x ∈ N^{-1/2}ℤ - N^{1/4}t} C_{N,t,x} P^ω(Nt, N^{3/4}t + N^{1/2}x) δ_x, where C_{N,t,x} = exp(N^{1/4}x + (N^{1/2} - N log cosh(N^{-1/4}))t). The field is extended to t ∈ ℝ_+ by linear interpolation. Fix an arbitrary T > 0.
global_context.target_theorem: The collection {U_N} is tight in C([0,T], X) for an explicit Banach space X of distributions (a weighted Hölder space) embedded in S'(ℝ). Any limit point lies in C((0,T], C(ℝ)) and coincides with the law of the Itô solution of the stochastic heat equation ∂_t U = (1/2)∂_xx U + √(8σ²/(1-4σ²)) U ξ with initial condition δ_0, where ξ is space-time white noise.

paper.title: Geometry of Gaussian free field sign clusters and random interlacements

global_context.setup: Consider an infinite, connected, locally finite weighted graph (G,λ) with associated random walk satisfying: uniform lower bound on transitions (p0), Ahlfors regularity of volume growth with exponent α, Green function decay with exponent β (2≤β<α), and weak sectional isoperimetry. Examples include ℤ^d (d≥3), products of fractals with ℤ, graphical Sierpinski carpets, and Cayley graphs of groups with polynomial growth.
global_context.target_theorem: For such graphs, the sign clusters of the Gaussian free field on G are strongly supercritical: with high probability there exist exactly two infinite sign clusters (one positive, one negative) and all finite sign clusters are tiny.

paper.title: On the Uniqueness of Global Multiple SLEs

global_context.setup: Let Ω ⊂ ℂ be a simply connected domain with 2N distinct points x₁,…,x₂ₙ on locally connected boundary segments, appearing in counterclockwise order. A link pattern α ∈ LP_N is a planar pairing of the indices {1,…,2N}. For α, consider families (η₁,…,η_N) of pairwise disjoint simple curves where η_j connects x_{a_j} and x_{b_j} for each pair {a_j,b_j} in α. A global N-SLE_κ associated to α is a probability measure on such families such that for each j, conditioned on the other curves, the law of η_j is chordal SLE_κ in the connected component of Ω \ ∪_{i≠j} η_i containing its endpoints.
global_context.target_theorem: Let κ ∈ (0,4] and let (Ω; x₁,…,x₂ₙ) be a polygon with N ≥ 1. For any α ∈ LP_N, there exists a unique global N-SLE_κ associated to α.

paper.title: Stochastic quantisation of Yang-Mills-Higgs in 3D

global_context.setup: We consider the Euclidean Yang–Mills–Higgs theory on the 3-torus T^3 with compact Lie group G and associated vector bundle V. The formal measure is exp(-S_YMH) with action S_YMH as in (1.2). Gauge transformations act as in (1.3). The stochastic quantization equation (1.4) with space-time white noise is studied. The state space S is a nonlinear metric space of distributions on E = g^3 ⊕ V, used as initial conditions for the deterministic and stochastic YMH flow. Gauge equivalence is extended to S, yielding a quotient space O of gauge orbits.
global_context.target_theorem: For every η∈(-1/2-κ,-1/2), where κ>0 is sufficiently small, there exists a complete (nonlinear) metric space (S,Σ) of E-valued distributions satisfying: (i) There is a canonical embedding S ↪ C^η(T^3,E) and there exists ν̄<0 such that C^{ν̄}(T^3,E) ↪ S densely. Furthermore, S is closed under scalar multiplication when viewed as a space of distributions. (ii) The deterministic DeTurck–YMH flow X ↦ F_t(X)∈C^∞(T^3,E) extends continuously to the closure of O_{t+s} in S for every s,t>0. It follows that t ↦ F_t(X) is well-posed for every X∈S and every sufficiently small t (depending on X) and the flow on gauge orbits X↦ ˜F_t∈O^∞(X) extends continuously to all of S for every t>0. One furthermore has lim_{t→0} Σ(F_t(X),X)=0 ∀ X∈S.

paper.title: CLAP: A New Algorithm for Promise CSPs

global_context.setup: Promise Constraint Satisfaction Problems (PCSPs) generalize CSPs. A PCSP is specified by two relational structures A and B with a homomorphism from A to B. The problem PCSP(A,B) asks: given an input structure I that maps homomorphically to A, decide if it maps homomorphically to B (promise: if I -> A then I -> B). The CLAP algorithm combines the Constraint Basic LP relaxation (BLP) and the Affine IP relaxation (AIP). Polymorphisms are operations that preserve the relations; they form a minion crucial for the algebraic approach.
global_context.target_theorem: \begin{thm}\label{thm:main1} Let $(\A,\B)$ be a PCSP template. Then, $\CLAP$ solves $\PCSP(\A,\B)$ if and only if there is a minion homomorphism from $\ourMinion$ to $\Pol(\A,\B)$. \end{thm}

local_context: \begin{rem} Consider the minion $\Mblpaip$ from~\cite{bgwz20} (cf.~Appendix~\ref{subsec:blp-aip}). A direct consequence of Example~\ref{ex:new2}, Theorem~\ref{thm:main1}, and~\cite[Lemma 5.4]{bgwz20} is that there is no minion homomorphism from $\Mblpaip$ to $\ourMinion$. On the other hand, the function \begin{align*} \vartheta:\ourMinion&\rightarrow\Mblpaip\\ (M,\bmu)&\mapsto (M\be_1,\bmu) \end{align*} is readily seen to be a minion homomorphism. It follows that $\CLAP$ solves any PCSP template solved by $\BLP+\AIP$ (as is also clear from the description of the two algorithms). \end{rem}

\begin{rem} Similar to~\cite{bgwz20}, the assumption in Theorem~\ref{thm:Hsymm} can be weakened as follows: Instead of requiring $H$-symmetric polymorphisms of arbitrarily large arity, it turns out to be enough requiring $H$-block-symmetric polymorphisms of arbitrarily large width, where the definition of an $H$-block-symmetric operation mirrors that of a block-symmetric operation in~\cite{bgwz20}. The proof of this possibly stronger result is very similar to that of Theorem~\ref{thm:Hsymm}. For completeness, we include it in Appendix~\ref{sec:block_sym_pol}. We point out that we do not know whether the condition in Theorem~\ref{thm:Hsymm} (or the possibly weaker condition based on $H$-block-symmetric polymorphisms) is necessary for tractability via CLAP, but we suspect it is not. \end{rem}

\begin{rem} A possibly stronger version of the $\CLAP$ algorithm consists in running $\BLP+\AIP$ (instead of just $\BLP$) at each iteration in the \textbf{for} loop in lines $5$--$9$ of Algorithm~\ref{alg:clap}, and then removing the additional \textbf{for} loop in lines $14$--$18$. This algorithm can be called C($\BLP+\AIP$). An analysis entirely analogous to the one presented in this paper shows that the power of C($\BLP+\AIP$) is captured by the minion $\tilde{\ourMinion}$ defined like $\ourMinion$ with the following difference: The $L$-ary elements of $\tilde{\ourMinion}$ are pairs $(M,N)$, where $M$ is as in $\ourMinion$ while $N$ is an integer matrix of the same size as $M$ taking the role of $\bmu$ (in particular, $N$ satisfies the \enquote{refinement condition} $\supp(N\be_i)\subseteq\supp(M\be_i)$ $\forall i\in\N$, analogous to $(c_4)$ in Definition~\ref{def:minion}). paper.title: Distributionally Robust Markov Decision Processes and their Connection to Risk Measures

global_context.setup: Finite-horizon Markov Decision Process with Borel state space E and action space A, ambiguity sets for disturbance distributions. The decision maker minimizes worst-case expected total cost over ambiguity sets. Assumptions include integrability of cost, continuity of transition kernels, and compactness of ambiguity sets.
global_context.target_theorem: Under integrability, continuity and compactness assumptions, the robust value function satisfies a dynamic programming recursion, and there exist deterministic optimal policies for both the decision maker and nature.

It is readily checked that Assumption \ref{ass:abstract_robust_real} is satisfied. Especially, one has constant bounding functions. In this example \eqref{eq:abstract_robust_minimax_randomize_counterexample_2} equals \begin{align*} & \inf_{a \in [0,1]} \sup_{p \in [0,1]} \E^p\big[c(x,a,T(x,a,Z))\big]= \inf_{a \in [0,1]} \sup_{p \in [0,1]} -(1-p)a^2 -p(a-1)^2\\ &= - \sup_{a \in [0,1]} \inf_{p \in [0,1]} (1-p)a^2 +p(a-1)^2= - \sup_{a \in [0,1]} \min\{a^2,(1-a)^2\} =-\frac14. \end{align*} If the controller chooses $\mu \sim \U(0,1)$, then \eqref{eq:abstract_robust_minimax_randomize_counterexample_1} must be less or equal than \begin{align*} \sup_{p \in [0,1]} \int_0^1 -(1-p)a^2 -p(a-1)^2 \, \dif a &= \sup_{p \in [0,1]} -\frac13 (1-p) - \frac13 p = -\frac13. \end{align*} Indeed we obtain here $ \sup_{p \in [0,1]}\inf_{a \in [0,1]} \E^p\big[c(x,a,T(x,a,Z))\big]=-\frac12$. The approach to interchange infimum and supremum through a linearization with randomized actions works when nature only observes the distribution and not the realization of the controller's action. global_context.setup: Planar Brownian motion (B_t) under P_{x_0} in a bounded simply connected domain D starting at x_0, with exit time τ. For ε>0, L_{x,ε}(τ) is the occupation time in the ball B(x,ε). For γ ∈ (0,2), define random measures μ_ε^γ(dx) = √(|log ε|) ε^{γ²/2} exp(γ √(ε^{-1}L_{x,ε}(τ))) dx and ν_ε^γ(dx,dt) = |log ε| ε^{-γ²/2} 1{ √(ε^{-1}L_{x,ε}(τ)) - γ log(1/ε) ∈ dt } dx.
global_context.target_theorem: For all γ ∈ (0,2), the sequences of random measures ν_ε^γ and μ_ε^γ converge as ε → 0 in probability for the topology of vague convergence on D × (ℝ ∪ {+∞}) and on D respectively towards Borel measures ν^γ and μ^γ.

paper.title: Anomalous scaling regime for one-dimensional Mott variable-range hopping

global_context.setup: The model is defined on a homogeneous Poisson process on ℝ with intensity ρ∈(0,∞), conditioned to have an atom at 0. Atoms are labelled …<ω₋₁<0<ω₁<… . Each ω_i carries an i.i.d. energy mark E_i uniformly distributed on [0,1]. For β,λ≥0, the conductance between ω_i and ω_j (i≠j) is c^{β,λ}(ω_i,ω_j)=exp(-|ω_i-ω_j|-βU(E_i,E_j)+λ(ω_j-ω_i)), where U(E_i,E_j)=min{E_i,E_j} if E_i·E_j<0 and 0 otherwise. The continuous-time random walk X^{β,λ} jumps from ω_i to ω_j at rate c^{β,λ}(ω_i,ω_j). The regime of interest is ρ<1.
global_context.target_theorem: For every ρ<1 and β,λ≥0, as n→∞, ℙ^{β,λ/n}((n^{-1}X_{n^{1+1/ρ}t})_{t≥0}∈·) converges weakly as probability measures on D([0,∞),ℝ) to the law of the continuous process Z^{β,λ} defined by Z^{β,λ}_t=(S^{β,λ})^{-1}(B_{H^{β,λ}_t}), where S^{β,λ} is a tilted two-sided ρ-stable Lévy process, B is a standard Brownian motion independent of S^{β,λ}, and H^{β,λ}_t=inf{s≥0:∫ℝ L^B_s(x) μ^{β,λ}(dx)>t} with μ^{β,λ} a certain random measure.

paper.title: The Brownian transport map

global_context.setup: Let γ be the Wiener measure on Wiener space. The Brownian transport map is constructed via the Föllmer process, pushing forward γ onto a target probability measure p on ℝ^d. p is assumed to be κ-log-concave for some κ ∈ ℝ, and S = diam(supp(p)).
global_context.target_theorem: Let p be a κ-log-concave measure for some κ ∈ ℝ and let S = diam(supp(p)). (i) If κS^2 ≥ 1 then the Brownian transport map between γ and p is an almost-sure contraction with constant 1/√κ. (ii) If κS^2 < 1 then the Brownian transport map between γ and p is an almost-sure contraction with constant ((e^{1-κS^2}+1)/2)^{1/2} S.

paper.title: Transitive closure in a polluted environment

global_context.setup: We consider a transitive closure process on an oriented graph. Initially, we have a graph G0 = (V, E0) of occupied edges on n vertices. Additional edges become occupied by transitivity: an open edge i -> j becomes occupied if there exists k such that i -> k and k -> j are occupied. Only edges in a random set of open edges can ever become occupied; all other edges are closed. In the specific case of Theorem 2, G0 is the unoriented linear graph L_n on vertices [n] with edges between consecutive vertices. Leftward and rightward edges (not in E0) are open independently with probabilities p_l and p_r, which may depend on n.
global_context.target_theorem: Assume G0 = L_n is the unoriented linear graph on V = [n] with edge set E0 consisting of all edges 1 <-> 2 <-> ... <-> n. Suppose that open leftward and rightward edges are chosen independently (from amongst those not in E0) with probabilities p_l and p_r. Fix a constant α > 0. Then there exist constants c ∈ (0,∞) and A ∈ (0,1) depending on α, and a constant C ∈ (0,∞), so that the following three statements hold. (1) When max{p_l, p_r} < c / √(log n), a.a.s. E∞ contains no edge longer than α log n. (2) When p_l < c / √(log n) and p_r > A, a.a.s. E∞ contains all open rightward edges longer than α log n, but no such leftward edge. (3) When min{p_l, p_r} > C log log n / √(log n), a.a.s. saturation occurs, E∞ = E0 ∪ E_open.

paper.title: Critical Brownian multiplicative chaos

global_context.setup: Thick points of planar Brownian motion/random walk are points that have been visited unusually often by the trajectory. The study of these points has a long history going back to the famous conjecture of Erd\H{o}s and Taylor \cite{erdos_taylor1960} on the leading order of the number of times a planar simple random walk visits the most visited site during the first $n$ steps. Since then, the understanding of these thick points has considerably improved. On the random walk side, \cite{dembo2001} settled Erd\H{o}s--Taylor conjecture and computed the number of thick points at the level of exponent, for random walk having symmetric increments with finite moments of all order. \cite{rosen2006,BassRosen2007}, and more recently \cite{jego2020}, streamlined the proof and extended these results to a wide class of planar random walk. On the Brownian motion side, \cite{bass1994} constructed random measures supported on the set of thick points. Their results concern only a partial range $\{a \in (0,1/2)\}$ of the thickness parameter $a$\footnote{$a$ is related to the parameter $\gamma$ in Gaussian multiplicative chaos theory by $a=\gamma^2/2$, so $a < 1/2$ corresponds to $\gamma < 1$.}. \cite{AidekonHuShi2018} and \cite{jegoGMC} extended simultaneously the results of \cite{bass1994} by building these random measures for the whole subcritical range $\{a \in (0,2)\}$. \cite{jegoRW} gave an axiomatic characterisation of these measures and showed that they describe the scaling limit of thick points of planar simple random walk for any fixed $a < 2$. All these aforementioned works are subcritical results. The aim of this paper is to extend the theory to the critical point $a=2$ by constructing a random measure supported by the thickest points of a planar Brownian trajectory. This enables us to formulate a precise conjecture on the convergence in distribution of the supremum of local times of planar random walk. Our construction is inspired by Gaussian multiplicative chaos theory (GMC), i.e. the study of random measures formally defined as the exponential of $\gamma$ times a log-correlated Gaussian field, such as the two-dimensional Gaussian free field (GFF), where $\gamma \geq 0$
global_context.target_theorem: \begin{theoremm}[Theorem 1.1 of \cite{jegoGMC}]\label{thmm:subcritical_measures} Let $\gamma \in (0,2)$. The sequence of random measures $m_\eps^\gamma$ converges as $\eps \to 0$ in probability for the topology of weak convergence on $D$ towards a Borel measure $m^\gamma$ called Brownian multiplicative chaos. \end{theoremm}

paper.title: Projective, Sparse, and Learnable Latent Position Network Models

global_context.setup: We consider latent position network models where each node i has a latent position Z_i in a metric space S. Edges Y_{ij} are independent Bernoulli with probability K(ρ(Z_i, Z_j)) for a decreasing, integrable kernel K. To achieve sparsity and projectivity, the latent positions are generated by a homogeneous Poisson point process over S with intensity measure ν, and we observe the induced graph on a growing observation window W_n ⊂ S. The model parameters include the kernel K and the intensity ν.

paper.title: Simultaneous high-probability bounds on the false discovery proportion in structured, regression, and online settings

global_context.setup: Testing a set of hypotheses \(\mathcal{H} = \{H_1, \dots, H_n\}\) with p-values that satisfy an independence condition. A nested path \(\Pi = \{\cR_k\}\) of rejection sets is constructed, along with an estimate \(\widehat{V}(\cR_k)\) of the number of false discoveries in \(\cR_k\). Define \(\overline{\text{FDP}}(\cR_k) = \lfloor c(\alpha)(a + \widehat{V}(\cR_k)) \rfloor / |\cR_k|\), where \(c(\alpha)\) are explicit, dimension-independent constants and \(a > 0\) is a regularization parameter.
global_context.target_theorem: Under independence of the p-values, the bound \(\overline{\text{FDP}}\) satisfies \(\PP{\text{FDP}(\cR) \leq \overline{\text{FDP}}(\cR) \text{ for all } \cR \in \Pi} \geq 1-\alpha\).

paper.title: Nonparametric Bayesian estimation in a multidimensional diffusion model with high frequency data

global_context.setup: We consider a multidimensional diffusion process with reflecting boundary on a bounded convex domain O in R^d, solving dX_t = ∇f(X_t) dt + √(2f(X_t)) dB_t + dℓ_t, where f is an unknown positive diffusivity function. Observations are high-frequency: X^N = (X_0, X_D, ..., X_{ND}) with sampling interval D = D_N → 0 and time horizon ND_N → ∞. The goal is nonparametric Bayesian estimation of f using Gaussian process priors.
global_context.target_theorem: The posterior distribution for f contracts around the true f0 at the minimax optimal rate over Hölder smoothness classes in L^2-loss, and the posterior mean is also minimax optimal.

local_context: \item[(i)] the integrated log-likelihood process is not too small with high probability; \item[(ii)] the existence of suitable tests with exponentially decaying type-II errors.

\end{itemize} Since the present high-frequency setting involves increasingly correlated observations, establishing (i) is much more involved than in the i.i.d. setting, where the log-likelihood tensorizes \cite{GGV00}.
In particular, we must deal with the full dependent log-likelihood ratio process as a whole.

paper.title: New assumptions for stability analysis in elliptic optimal control problems

global_context.setup: Study optimal control problem (P) minimizing J(u)=∫_Ω L(x,y_u,u) dx subject to u ∈ Uad = {u ∈ L^2(Ω): u_a ≤ u ≤ u_b a.e.}, with semilinear elliptic state equation -div(A∇y)+b·∇y+f(x,y)=u in Ω, y=0 on Γ. Ω⊂ℝ^n (n=2,3) bounded Lipschitz, A uniformly elliptic, b ∈ L^p (p≥3 if n=3, p>2 if n=2), f satisfies further conditions. L may depend linearly on u or not at all.
global_context.target_theorem: \begin{theorem} Let $\bar u$ be a (strong or not) local minimizer of \Pb, then there exist two unique elements $\bar y, \bar\varphi \in H_0^1(\Omega) \cap C(\bar\Omega)$ such that \begin{align} & \left\{\begin{array}{l} \mathcal{A}\bar y + f(x,\bar y) = \bar u \text{ in } \Omega,\\ \bar y = 0\text{ on } \Gamma,\end{array}\right. \label{E3.7}\\ &\left\{\begin{array}{l}\displaystyle \mathcal{A}^*\bar\varphi + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}(x,\bar y)\bar\varphi = \frac{\partial L}{\partial y}(x,\bar y,\bar u) \text{ in } \Omega,\\ \bar\varphi = 0\text{ on } \Gamma,\end{array}\right. \label{E3.8}\\ &\int_\Omega(\bar\varphi + g)(u - \bar u)\, dx \ge 0 \quad \forall u \in \Uad. \label{E3.9} \end{align} \label{T3.2} \end{theorem}

local_context: u) \text{ in } \Omega,\\ \bar\varphi = 0\text{ on } \Gamma,\end{array}\right. \label{E3.8}\\ &\int_\Omega(\bar\varphi + g)(u - \bar u)\, dx \ge 0 \quad \forall u \in \Uad. \label{E3.9} \end{align} \label{T3.2} \end{theorem}

The derivation of sufficient second order conditions for local optimality is more delicate. First we introduce the cone of critical directions on which we formulate the necessary second order conditions for optimality: if $\bar u \in \Uad$ is a local minimizer of \Pb we define \[ C_{\bar u} = \{v \in L^2(\Omega) : J'(\bar u)v = 0\ \text{ and $v$ satisfies the sign conditions \eqref{E3.10}}\}, \] \begin{equation} v(x)\left\{\begin{array}{cl}\ge 0&\text{if } \bar u(x) = u_a,\\\le 0&\text{if } \bar u(x) = u_b.\end{array}\right. \label{E3.10} \end{equation} As usual, from \eqref{E3.9} we deduce that $(\bar\varphi+g)(x)v(x) \ge 0$ for almost all $x \in \Omega$ if $v \in L^2(\Omega)$ satisfies \eqref{E3.10}. Therefore, the condition $J'(\bar u)v = 0$ for $v$ satisfying \eqref{E3.10} is only possible if $v(x) = 0$ for almost every $x \in \Omega$ such that $(\bar\varphi+g)(x) \neq 0$. Therefore, $C_{\bar u}$ can be written \[ C_{\bar u} = \{v \in L^2(\Omega) : \text{satisfying \eqref{E3.10} and } v(x) = 0 \text{ if } |(\bar\varphi+g)(x)| > 0\}. \]

It is well known that every local minimizer $\bar u$ satisfies the second order necessary optimality condition: $J''(\bar u)v^2 \ge 0$ for all $v \in C_{\bar u}$; see, for instance, \cite{Casas-Troltzsch2012}. However, based on $C_{\bar u}$ it is not possible to get sufficient second order conditions for local optimality. The reader is referred to \cite{Dunn98} for a counterexample. A procedure suggested by several authors consists in extending the cone of critical directions $C_{\bar u}$; see \cite{DHPY95,Dunn95,Maurer81,Maurer-Zowe79}. Two possible extensions of $C_{\bar u}$ seem natural after the above comments: for $\tau > 0$ we define the extended cones \begin{align*} &D^\tau_{\bar u} = \{v \in L^2(\Omega) : \text{satisfying \eqref{E3.10} and } v(x) = 0 \text{ if } |(\bar\varphi + g)(x)| > \tau\},\\ &G^\tau_{\bar u} = \{v \in L^2(\Omega) : \text{satisfying \eqref{E3.10} and } J'(\bar u)v \le \tau\|z_v\|_{L^1(\Omega)}\}. \end{align*} On any of these cones we can formulate sufficient second order conditions for local optimality. Obviously, both are extensions of $C_{\bar u}$. In \cite{Casas-Mateos2020}, the authors introduced the cone $C_{\bar u}^\tau = D^\tau_{\bar u} \cap G^\tau_{\bar u}$, which is also an extension of $C_{\bar u}$. They proved that the first order optimality conditions \eqref{E3.7}--\eqref{E3.9} along with the condition \begin{equation} \exists \delta > 0 \text{ such that } J''(\bar u)v^2 \ge \delta\|z_v\|^2_{L^2(\Omega)}\quad \forall v \in C_{\bar u}^\tau \label{E3.11} \end{equation} imply the existence of $\kappa > 0$ and $\varepsilon > 0$ such that \begin{equation} J(\bar u) + \frac{\kappa}{2}\|y_u - \bar y\|^2_{L^2(\Omega)} \le J(u) \ \ \forall u \in \Uad \text{ such that } \|y_u - \bar y\|_{C(\bar\Omega)} < \varepsilon. \label{E3.12} \end{equation} Actually, the proof of \cite{Casas-Mateos2020} was carried out for a parabolic control problem with $g = 0$. However, the same proof works for the elliptic case and $g \neq 0$. Here, we formulate a new assumption leading to the same result \eqref{E3.12} as \eqref{E3.11} does.
