paper.title: First passage percolation with long-range correlations and applications to random Schrödinger operators

global_context.setup: First passage percolation on the lattice Z^d, d>=2, with edge set E_d and random passage times (t_e) under a family of probability measures (P^u). The FPP distance d^ω(x,y) is the infimum over paths of the sum of passage times. The time constant μ_u(x) is defined as the almost sure limit of d^ω(0,nx)/n. We consider a general class of ergodic models with long-range correlations, requiring conditions P1 (ergodicity), P2 (monotonicity), and P3/P3' (decay of correlations) as specified in Section 2.
global_context.target_theorem: \begin{theorem} \label{The:main} Suppose that $(\bbP^u)_{u\in I}$ satisfies {\bf P2} and either {\bf P3} or {\bf P3'}. Further, assume \begin{equation} \label{intro:BLnotconnectedtoB2L} \liminf_{L\rightarrow\infty}\sup_{x\in{\bbZ^d}}\bbP^u\Big(Q(x,L)\longleftrightarrow Q(x,2L)^c\text{ in }\{e\in{E_d}:\,t^\om_e=0\}\Big)=0, \quad \text{for all }u\in{I}. \end{equation} Then, for all $u\in{I}$ and $\delta>1$, there exist constants $c_8,c_9,C_{\mathrm{fpp}}>0$ such that for all $x\in{\bbZ^d\setminus\{0\}}$, \begin{equation} \label{intro:boundonT0nx} \bbP^u\Big(d^{\omega}(0,nx)\leq C_{\mathrm{fpp}}n\Big) \leq c_8 \exp\Big(-c_9 \, g_P^{(\delta)}(n)\Big), \qquad \forall n\in \bbN. \end{equation} In particular, if in addition $(\bbP^u)_{u\in I}$ satisfies {\bf P1}, then, for each $u\in{I}$ such that \eqref{eq:boundonmement} holds, we have that $\mu_u(x)\geq C_{\mathrm{fpp}}>0$ for all $x\in\bbZ^d\setminus\{0\}.$ \end{theorem}

paper.title: Algebraic approach to promise constraint satisfaction

global_context.setup: The promise constraint satisfaction problem (PCSP) extends the CSP framework: given an instance guaranteed to have a homomorphism to a template A, the goal is to find a homomorphism to a template B. This captures approximation versions of problems like graph coloring, where for cliques K_k and K_l with k < l, PCSP(K_k, K_l) corresponds to finding an l-coloring of a k-colorable graph. The algebraic approach uses polymorphisms to study complexity.
global_context.target_theorem: For any $k\geq 3$, it is $\NP$-hard to find a~$(2k-1)$-colouring of a~given $k$-colourable graph.

paper.title: Parameterized Approximation Schemes for Steiner Trees with Small Number of Steiner Vertices

global_context.setup: Given an edge-weighted undirected graph G=(V,E), terminal set R ⊆ V, parameter p (integer) representing the maximum number of Steiner vertices allowed, and ε > 0, the goal is to compute a (1+ε)-approximation to the cheapest Steiner tree T with at most p Steiner vertices (i.e., p ≥ |V(T) \ R|). The Steiner Tree problem is APX-hard and W[2]-hard for parameter p.
global_context.target_theorem: There is an algorithm for ST, which given an edge-weighted undirected graph G=(V,E), terminal set R ⊆ V, ε > 0, and an integer p, computes a (1+ε)-approximation to the cheapest Steiner tree T ⊆ G with p ≥ |V(T) \ R| in time 2^{O(p^2/ε^4)} poly(n).

paper.title: The $s$-weak order and $s$-permutahedra I: combinatorics and lattice structure

global_context.setup: The s-weak order is a partial order on s-decreasing trees, defined as the inclusion order of their s-tree inversions, where s is a weak composition (sequence of non-negative integers).
global_context.target_theorem: The s-weak order is a semidistributive and congruence uniform lattice.

local_context: The \defn{$s$-Tamari poset} is the restriction of the $s$-weak order to the set of $s$-Tamari trees. \end{definition}

In the case of permutations ($s = (1,1,\dots)$), $s$-Tamari trees are in correspondence with $231$-avoiding permutations. If the signature $s$ has no zeros, then $s$-decreasing trees correspond to $121$-avoiding \mbox{$s$-permutations}, and $s$-Tamari trees to the subset of those that are $231$-avoiding. paper.title: Posets arising from decompositions of objects in a monoidal category

global_context.setup: We consider a monoidal category (C, ⊔) with an initial object 0. For an object X in C, we define the poset Sub(X,⊔) of ⊔-complemented subobjects, the poset D(X) of ⊔-decompositions, and the poset PD(X) of partial ⊔-decompositions. We also define complexes of frames and partial bases, and ordered versions of these posets and complexes.
global_context.target_theorem: \begin{theorem} [{Sadofschi Costa}] \label{thm:sadofschi} Let $X$ be a free group of finite rank $n$. Then the posets $\F(X)$ and $\PB(X)$ are Cohen-Macaulay of dimension $n-1$. \end{theorem}

paper.title: The balancing number and list balancing number of some graph classes

global_context.setup: Let $G$ be a graph. A 2-list edge coloring of $K_n$ assigns each edge $e$ a list $L(e)\subseteq\{r,b\}$; then $R=\{e: r\in L(e)\}$, $B=\{e: b\in L(e)\}$. The list balancing number $\operatorname{lbal}(n,G)$ is the smallest $k$ such that every coloring with $|R|,|B|>k$ contains a balanced copy of $G$ (half its edges in each color). For $G$, define $\mathcal{H}(G)$ as the family of subgraphs of $G$ with exactly $\lfloor e(G)/2\rfloor$ edges and no isolated vertices. For a family $\mathcal{F}$, $\operatorname{ex}(n,\mathcal{F})$ is the maximum number of edges in an $n$-vertex graph containing no member of $\mathcal{F}$.
global_context.target_theorem: Let $G=G(V,E)$ be a graph and $n\geq |V|$ an integer. Then $\operatorname{lbal}(n,G) \leq \frac{1}{2}\binom{n}{2}+\left\lceil \frac{\operatorname{ex}(n,\mathcal{H}(G))}{2} \right\rceil$.

paper.title: Bounds and extremal graphs for total dominating identifying codes

global_context.setup: Let G be a finite undirected graph of order n. An identifying code C ⊆ V(G) is a dominating set such that for any distinct vertices u,v, the sets N[u]∩C and N[v]∩C differ. A total dominating identifying code is an identifying code that is also a total dominating set, i.e., every vertex has a neighbor in C. The smallest size of a total dominating identifying code of G is denoted γ_t^ID(G). A graph is identifiable if it admits an identifying code.
global_context.target_theorem: If G is a connected identifiable graph of order n (thus n ≥ 3), then we have γ_t^ID(G) ≤ n-1, unless G is P_3 (and γ_t^ID(P_3)=3).

paper.title: Graph Classes and Forbidden Patterns on Three Vertices

global_context.setup: Patterns on three vertices are ordered trigraphs on three vertices with edges, non-edges, and undecided edges. A graph G belongs to the class C_F for a family F of patterns if there exists an ordering of V(G) such that no induced subgraph on three vertices matches any pattern in F (respecting the order). Basic operations include mirror, complement, and pattern split rule.
global_context.target_theorem: There are exactly 22 different graph classes (up to complement) defined by sets of forbidden patterns on three vertices, and 20 of these can be recognized in linear time.

paper.title: More on Nosal's spectral theorem: Books and $4$-cycles

global_context.setup: Let G be a graph with m edges. The spectral radius λ(G) is the largest eigenvalue of its adjacency matrix. A book of size k is a collection of k triangles sharing a common edge.
global_context.target_theorem: Every m-edge graph G with λ(G) > √m contains at least (1/144)√m triangles sharing a common edge.

paper.title: Modular Relations of the Tutte Symmetric Function

global_context.setup: The Tutte symmetric function XB maps from the t-extended Hopf algebra G[t] of vertex-labelled graphs to symmetric functions. An element L in G[t] is a modular relation for XB if for any graph G, Ext(L;G) lies in Ker(XB). Write L in Tutte standard form as L = ∑ c_i (1+t)^{n_i} H_i with H_i graphs on vertex set [n] and distinct pairs (n_i, H_i). For a partition π of [n], e_H(π) is the number of edges of H with both endpoints in the same block.
global_context.target_theorem: Let L = ∑_{i=1}^k c_i (1+t)^{n_i} H_i be an element of G[t] with each H_i a graph with vertex set [n], written in Tutte standard form. Then L is a modular relation for XB if and only if for every partition π ⊢ [n] we have B(L;π) := ∑_{i=1}^k c_i (1+t)^{n_i + e_{H_i}(π)} = 0.

local_context: Regardless of which case was used, we relabel again to restore the vertices to $h_1 < h_2 < h_3$. In all cases, we will have subtracted elements of $I$, $S_1$, and $S_2$ from $H$ to obtain a linear combination of three other graphs $H_1$, $H_2$, and $H_3$, and it is easy to check that indeed $H \prec H_1$, $H \prec H_2$ and $H \prec H_3$ all hold. Now, as long as any of the $H_i$ still remain dull, we may apply this process again to each such graph. Since each application of this process produces graphs that are strictly larger in a finite poset, this process must terminate after a finite number of applications, necessarily in a linear combination of bright star forests.

Returning to the main proof, we have now demonstrated that for any $G \in \mathbb{G}[t]$ with vertex set $[n]$, we may subtract elements of $I$, $S_1$, and $S_2$ to write $G$ as a linear combination $L_3$ of bright star forests.

Now, for each integer partition $\lm \vdash n$, we choose a canonical bright star forest with vertex set $[n]$ by defining $R_{\lm}$ to be the star forest such that the stars are induced by the vertex sets $\{1, \dots, \lm_1\}, \{\lm_1+1 \dots, \lm_1+\lm_2\}, \dots, \{|V(G)|-\lm_l+1, \dots, |V(G)|\}$, and each star is rooted at its largest vertex. By subtracting further elements of $I$, we may rewrite $L_3$ so that every graph that occurs is one of the $R_{\lm}$, and thus $L_3$ may be written as $L_4 = \sum_{\lm, k} c_{\lm, k}(1+t)^kR_{\lm}$ for some $c_{\lm, k} \in \mathbb{C}[t]$.

paper.title: The counting version of a problem of Erdős

global_context.setup: A set A ⊆ ℕ possesses property P_h if there are no distinct elements a0, a1, ..., ah in A with a0 dividing the product a1a2...ah. Let H_h(n) = |P_h([n])| be the number of subsets of [n] possessing property P_h. Define T(n) = ∏_{√n < p ≤ n, p prime} (⌊n/p⌋+1).
global_context.target_theorem: There exist positive constants c1 and c2 such that, for the number of those subsets of [n] that possess property P_2, we have T(n)·e^{c1 n^{2/3}/log n} ≤ H_2(n) ≤ T(n)·e^{c2 n^{2/3}/log n}, if n is sufficiently large. Let h≥3 be an integer. For large enough n, the number of subsets of [n] possessing property P_h satisfies T(n)·e^{√n}e^{-11√n log log n/log n} ≤ H_h(n) ≤ T(n)·e^{√n}e^{4√n log log n/log n}, where T(n):=∏_{√n<p≤n, p prime} (⌊n/p⌋+1).

local_context: We claim that $A=A_1\cup A_2$ possesses property $\mathcal{P}_2$. If $a_0\in A_1$, then there is a prime $p\in (\sqrt{n},n]$ such that $p\mid a_0 $ and $p\nmid a$, if $a\in A\setminus \{a_0\}$, so $a_0\nmid a_1a_2$. If $a_0\in A_2$, then $a_0=pqr$ for three primes $p,q,r\in (n^{1/3}/2,n^{1/3})$. Assume that $a_0\mid a_1a_2$ for some $a_1,a_2\in A\setminus \{a_0\}$. We can assume that $a_1$ is divisible by at least two of the primes $p,q,r$, for instance, $pq\mid a_1$. If $a_1\in A_2$, then this contradicts the linearity of $G$. If $a_1\in A_1$, then $a_1$ has one prime factor larger than $\sqrt{n}$ and two prime factors ($p$ and $q$) from $(n^{1/3}/2,n^{1/3})$, which is a contradiction again.
Hence, each set that can be obtained as $A=A_1\cup A_2$ possesses property $\mathcal{P}_2$. Note that for different pairs $(A_1,A_2)$ we get different sets $A=A_1\cup A_2$.

The number of choices for $A_1$ is $$T(n)=\prod\limits_{\substack{\sqrt{n}<p\leq n, \\ p \text{ prime} }} (\lfloor n/p \rfloor +1),$$ since for each prime $p\in (\sqrt{n},n]$ we can include in $A_1$ either one of the $[n/p]$ multiples of $p$ (up to $n$) or none of them, the choices for different primes are independent from each other.

paper.title: Splines on Cayley Graphs of the Symmetric Group

global_context.setup: Let Γ be a connected simple graph on vertex set [n], edges identified with transpositions in S_n. The Cayley graph Cay(S_n, E(Γ)) is edge-labeled by ideals (t_i - t_j) for each edge {i,j} in Γ. The ring of splines M_Γ consists of functions f: S_n → C[t_1,...,t_n] such that for each edge (w, w(i j)), f(w) - f(w(i j)) ∈ (t_{w(i)} - t_{w(j)}). This generalizes GKM constructions for equivariant cohomology of flag varieties, Hessenberg varieties, and permutohedral varieties.
global_context.target_theorem: \begin{customthm}{C}\label{intthm:connectedness} Let $\Gamma$ be a connected simple graph. The following are equivalent: \begin{itemize} \item[1)] The graph $\Gamma$ is $k$-connected. \item[2)] For all $d<k$, the symmetric function $\lrep{\Gamma}_d$ corresponds to a trivial representation. \item[3)] For all $d<k$, the $d$-th graded piece of $\splines{\Gamma}$ is isomorphic to the $d$-th graded piece of $\splines{K_n}$, where $K_n$ is the complete graph on $n$ vertices. \end{itemize} \end{customthm}

\end{itemize} \end{customthm} Geometrically, the $d$-th graded piece of $\splines{K_n}$ is isomorphic to the $2d$-th equivariant cohomology of the full flag variety, and is thus spanned by equivariant Schubert classes whose spline formula is given in \cite{billey1999schub_formula}.

paper.title: Sparsification of Binary CSPs

global_context.setup: A binary constraint satisfaction problem (CSP) instance consists of variables V, a finite domain D, a set of constraints Π each being a pair of distinct variables and a predicate P: D^2 → {0,1}, and positive weights w: Π → ℝ_+. An ε-sparsifier is a re-weighted sub-instance with a subset of constraints that approximates the value of every assignment within (1±ε).
global_context.target_theorem: \begin{theorem}[\protect{\cite[Theorem~3.7]{Fil}}]\label{thm:Boolbinary} Let $P:\{0,1\}^2\to\{0,1\}$ be a binary Boolean predicate. Let $0<\eps<1$. \begin{enumerate} \item If $P$ is a singleton then there exists an instance $I$ of $\CSPP$ such that every $\eps$-sparsifier of $I$ has $\Omega(n^2)$ constraints. \item Otherwise, for every instance $I$ of $\CSPP$ there exists an $\eps$-sparsifier of $I$ with $O(\eps^{-2}n)$ constraints. \end{enumerate} \end{theorem}

Recall that Sidorenko's conjecture asserts that $H$ satisfies~(\ref{eq:A1}) if and only if $H$ is bipartite. Similarly, the Forcing conjecture asserts that $H$ satisfies~(\ref{eq:A2}) if and only if $H$ is bipartite and contains a cycle. In both cases, the $\Rightarrow$ direction is easy. Let us recall that the reason why at least one cycle is required for the Forcing conjecture is that the homomorphism density of any forest $H$ in any $p$-regular graphon (whether constant-$p$, or not) is $p^{e(H)}$. The other direction in both conjectures is open, despite being known in many special cases, see~\cite{MR3667583,Lov:Sidorenko,MR3456171,Conlon2010,Hat:Siderenko,Li,SzegedyEntropySidorenko,ConLee:Sidorenko,CoKiLe:AdvancesOnSidorenko}.

Because all the properties we investigate in this section strengthen~(\ref{eq:A1}), we are concerned only with bipartite graphs throughout. The only exception is Remark~\ref{rem:reversedcutdistsubgraph} which addresses a possible <<converse>> definition of cut distance identifying properties.

paper.title: The (theta, wheel)-free graphs Part II: structure theorem

global_context.setup: Graphs are finite and simple. A theta is formed by three internally node-disjoint chordless paths of length at least 2 between two vertices, with no edges between the paths except at the ends. A wheel consists of a hole (chordless cycle of length at least 4) and a vertex with at least 3 neighbors on the hole. A graph is (theta, wheel)-free if it contains no induced theta and no induced wheel. A clique cutset is a clique whose removal disconnects the graph. A 2-join is a partition of the vertex set into two sets X1, X2, each containing two disjoint nonempty cliques A_i, B_i, such that all edges between X1 and X2 are between A1 and A2 and between B1 and B2, with additional conditions on sizes and connectivity. A P-graph is a graph that can be partitioned into a clique K and the line graph L(R) of a triangle-free chordless graph R, with specific attachment rules.
global_context.target_theorem: If G is (theta, wheel)-free, then G is a line graph of a triangle-free chordless graph or a P-graph, or G has a clique cutset or a 2-join.

paper.title: The Price of Connectivity in Fair Division

global_context.setup: Indivisible goods are vertices of a connected undirected graph G. There are n agents with additive utilities. A connected allocation assigns each agent a bundle that forms a connected subgraph. The maximin share (MMS) of an agent is her max-min value over all partitions of all goods into n bundles. The graph-specific maximin share (G-MMS) is defined similarly but only over connected partitions. The price of connectivity (PoC) for G and n is sup_u MMS(u,n)/G-MMS(G,u,n).
global_context.target_theorem: Let $n$ be any positive integer and $G$ be any graph. If $n=2$ (and $G$ is arbitrary), or if $G$ is a tree (and $n$ is arbitrary), then there always exists a connected allocation that gives each agent at least $1/\text{PoC}(G,n)$ times her MMS. Moreover, the factor $1/\text{PoC}(G,n)$ is tight in both cases.

paper.title: Topological methods in zero-sum Ramsey theory

global_context.setup: Let p be a prime, H a p-uniform hypergraph with vertex set V. A Z/p-coloring is a map c: V -> Z/p. A hyperedge e in H is zero-sum if sum_{v in e} c(v)=0. The box complex B(H) is a simplicial complex on V x Z/p: σ = ∪_{i in Z/p} (A_i x {i}) ∈ B(H) if for all choices a_i ∈ A_i, the set {a_0,...,a_{p-1}} is a hyperedge in H. B(H) carries a free Z/p-action by shifting the Z/p coordinate. S^{2p-3} is the sphere with a free Z/p-action via multiplication by roots of unity on coordinates.
global_context.target_theorem: Let p ≥ 2 be a prime, and let H be a p-uniform hypergraph. If there is no Z/p-equivariant map B(H) → S^{2p-3}, then for any Z/p-coloring of H there is a zero-sum hyperedge in H.

paper.title: Minor-closed graph classes with bounded layered pathwidth

global_context.setup: We consider finite simple graphs. A path decomposition of a graph G is a sequence of bags (B1,...,Bn) of vertices such that every vertex appears in a contiguous set of bags and for each edge there is a bag containing both endpoints; its width is max|Bi|-1. Pathwidth pw(G) is the minimum width. A layering is a partition (V0,V1,...,Vt) of V(G) such that every edge has endpoints in layers with indices differing by at most 1. Layered pathwidth lpw(G) is the minimum integer ℓ such that there exist a path decomposition and a layering where each bag contains at most ℓ vertices in each layer. A minor is obtained by deleting and contracting edges. A class is minor-closed if it is closed under taking minors. An apex-forest is a graph that becomes a forest after deleting one vertex.
global_context.target_theorem: A minor-closed class of graphs has bounded layered pathwidth if and only if some apex-forest is not in the class.

paper.title: Graph Classes and Forbidden Patterns on Three Vertices

global_context.setup: Patterns on three vertices are ordered trigraphs on three vertices with edges, non-edges, and undecided edges. A graph G belongs to the class C_F for a family F of patterns if there exists an ordering of V(G) such that no induced subgraph on three vertices matches any pattern in F (respecting the order). Basic operations include mirror, complement, and pattern split rule.
global_context.target_theorem: There are exactly 22 different graph classes (up to complement) defined by sets of forbidden patterns on three vertices, and 20 of these can be recognized in linear time.

paper.title: The Moran forest

global_context.setup: Consider a Markov chain on directed graphs on {1,...,n}. At each step, choose an ordered pair of distinct vertices (u,v) uniformly, disconnect v from all neighbors, and add edge u→v. The stationary distribution is supported on non-empty rooted forests on {1,...,n}, called the Moran forest.
global_context.target_theorem: Let T be a uniform rooted tree on {1,...,n-1}. Build a forest F on {1,...,n} by: 1) Remove all decreasing edges from T (edges u→v pointing away from the root with u>v). 2) Add vertex n and connect it to a uniformly chosen vertex of T. 3) Relabel vertices by a uniform permutation of {1,...,n}. Then F has the law of the Moran forest MF_n.

paper.title: Quasi-random words and limits of word sequences

global_context.setup: Words are sequences over alphabet {0,1}. A word w of length n is (d,ε)-uniform if for every interval I ⊆ [n], the number of 1's in I equals d|I| ± εn. For a word w, let ‖w‖_1 be its number of 1's. For a pattern u ∈ {0,1}^ℓ, let (w choose u) count subsequences of w equal to u. Consider a sequence (w_n) of words w_n ∈ {0,1}^n with ‖w_n‖_1 = d n + o(n) for some d ∈ [0,1].
global_context.target_theorem: For a sequence (w_n)_{n→∞} of words w_n ∈ {0,1}^n such that ‖w_n‖_1 = d n + o(n) for some d ∈ [0,1], the following are equivalent: (Uniformity) (w_n) is (d,o(1))-uniform. (Counting) For all ℓ ∈ ℕ and all u ∈ {0,1}^ℓ, (w_n choose u) = d^{‖u‖_1}(1-d)^{ℓ-‖u‖_1} (n choose ℓ) + o(n^ℓ). (Minimizer) For all u ∈ {0,1}^3, (w_n choose u) = d^{‖u‖_1}(1-d)^{3-‖u‖_1} (n choose 3) + o(n^3). (Exponential sums) For any fixed k ∈ ℕ, k ≠ 0, (1/n)∑_{j∈[n]} w_n[j] exp((2π i/n) k j) = o(1). (Equidistribution) For every Lipschitz f: ℝ/ℤ → ℂ, (1/n)∑_{j∈[n]} w_n[j] f(j/n) = d ∫_{ℝ/ℤ} f + o(1). (Cayley graph) For all u ∈ {0,1}^3, the number of induced u-walks in Γ(w_n) is d^{‖u‖_1}(1-d)^{3-‖u‖_1} 2n (n choose 3) + o(n^4).

global_context.target_theorem: For p<2, E_p(A) ≥ 2(N-d) p^{-p/2} (2-p)^{(p-2)/2}. This bound is sharp for d ≤ N ≤ 2d and p=1.

paper.title: Plane partitions of shifted double staircase shape

global_context.setup: We consider shifted plane partitions of shifted double staircase shape. For integers 0 ≤ k ≤ n, the shifted double staircase shape is λ = (n,n-1,…,1) + (k,k-1,…,1). For an integer m ≥ 0, let SPP^m(λ) denote the set of shifted plane partitions of shape λ with entries at most m. A shifted plane partition is a filling of the shifted Young diagram of λ with nonnegative integers weakly decreasing along rows and down columns.
global_context.target_theorem: Let 0 ≤ k ≤ n, and let λ = (n,n-1,…,1) + (k,k-1,…,1) be a shifted double staircase shape. Then #SPP^m(λ) = ∏_{1 ≤ i ≤ j ≤ n} (m+i+j-1)/(i+j-1) ∏_{1 ≤ i ≤ j ≤ k} (m+i+j)/(i+j).

local_context: In \cref{thm:sds} we give a product formula for plane partitions of \dfn{shifted staircase shape}, that is, the plane partitions in $\mathcal{SPP}^{m}(\lambda)$ where $\lambda \coloneqq (n,n-1,\ldots,1) + (k,k-1,\ldots,1)$ for $0 \leq k \leq n$. Note that the case $k=0$ of this family is the shifted staircases, and the cases $k=n-1,n$ are special cases of the shifted trapezoids. Thus, this family includes the symmetric and the symmetric, self-complementary boxed plane partitions (and in some sense interpolates between them).

It is interesting to note that, unlike the case of the shifted trapezoids, the entries of Stembridge's Pfaffian in \cref{thm:stembridge_pfaffian} do not simplify for the shifted double staircases, and it thus seems hopeless to try to explicitly evaluate this Pfaffian.

Our \cref{thm:flashlight} is an extension of the shifted double staircase product formula to a more general lozenge tiling region. As mentioned, we prove this formula by extending the work of Ciucu~\cite{ciucu2020correlation}, and in particular applying the variant of Kuo condensation he developed there. One small note about deducing \cref{thm:sds} from \cref{thm:flashlight}: notice that we have the requirement $y>0$ in \cref{thm:flashlight} (see \cref{rem:y_zero}), which means that technically the case $k=n$ of \cref{thm:sds} does not follow from \cref{thm:flashlight}. However, this is okay because as mentioned the case $k=n$ of \cref{thm:sds} is a special case of Proctor's shifted trapezoid formula~\cite{proctor1983trapezoid}, and so is known.

Except for the general $d$ case of the arithmetic progression shapes, all the previously discussed shapes with product formulas have a representation theoretic interpretation. In this paper we do not give such an interpretation for the shifted double staircases. However, as mentioned in~\cref{rem:okada}, Okada's recent paper~\cite{okada2020intermediate} does give a character-theoretic extension of the shifted double staircase product formula; in particular, he obtains $q$-analogs of this product formula. paper.title: Quasi-random words and limits of word sequences

global_context.setup: Words are sequences over alphabet {0,1}. A word w of length n is (d,ε)-uniform if for every interval I ⊆ [n], the number of 1's in I equals d|I| ± εn. For a word w, let ‖w‖_1 be its number of 1's. For a pattern u ∈ {0,1}^ℓ, let (w choose u) count subsequences of w equal to u. Consider a sequence (w_n) of words w_n ∈ {0,1}^n with ‖w_n‖_1 = d n + o(n) for some d ∈ [0,1].
global_context.target_theorem: For a sequence (w_n)_{n→∞} of words w_n ∈ {0,1}^n such that ‖w_n‖_1 = d n + o(n) for some d ∈ [0,1], the following are equivalent: (Uniformity) (w_n) is (d,o(1))-uniform. (Counting) For all ℓ ∈ ℕ and all u ∈ {0,1}^ℓ, (w_n choose u) = d^{‖u‖_1}(1-d)^{ℓ-‖u‖_1} (n choose ℓ) + o(n^ℓ). (Minimizer) For all u ∈ {0,1}^3, (w_n choose u) = d^{‖u‖_1}(1-d)^{3-‖u‖_1} (n choose 3) + o(n^3). (Exponential sums) For any fixed k ∈ ℕ, k ≠ 0, (1/n)∑_{j∈[n]} w_n[j] exp((2π i/n) k j) = o(1). (Equidistribution) For every Lipschitz f: ℝ/ℤ → ℂ, (1/n)∑_{j∈[n]} w_n[j] f(j/n) = d ∫_{ℝ/ℤ} f + o(1). (Cayley graph) For all u ∈ {0,1}^3, the number of induced u-walks in Γ(w_n) is d^{‖u‖_1}(1-d)^{3-‖u‖_1} 2n (n choose 3) + o(n^4).

paper.title: An FPT Algorithm for the Embeddability of Graphs Into Two-Dimensional Simplicial Complexes

global_context.setup: Given a graph G and a two-dimensional simplicial complex C, decide whether G admits a topological embedding into C. Let c be the number of simplices in C and n be the total number of vertices and edges in G. The problem is NP-hard, even when C is a surface.
global_context.target_theorem: One can solve the embeddability problem of graphs into 2-dimensional simplicial complexes in time O(2^{poly(c)} ⋅ n^2), where c is the number of simplices of the input 2-complex and n is the total number of vertices and edges of the input graph.

(General position means that the only crossings involve exactly the relative interiors of two edges.)

\end{document} Many hard graph problems become tractable when restricting the input to some graph class. The two central questions are ``for which graph classes does a graph problem become tractable'' and ``for which graph classes does it stay computationally hard?'' Ideally, we wish to answer these questions for a large set of problems simultaneously instead of considering individual problems one by one.

Graph width parameters~\cite{DJP19,Gu17,HOSG08,KLM09,Va12} help to make such results possible. A graph class has {\em bounded} width if there is a constant~$c$ such that the width of all its members is at most~$c$. As we discuss below, there are several meta-theorems that provide sufficient
x.anchor_hint: As we discuss below, there are several meta-theorems that provide sufficient.

paper.title: Renitent lines

global_context.setup: Let AG(2,q) be the desarguesian affine plane over F_q, q a power of prime p. Let T be a multiset of points in AG(2,q). For a direction (d) (point on the line at infinity), the affine lines with slope d form a parallel class. A direction (d) is (q-λ)-uniform w.r.t. T if at least q-λ lines with slope d meet T in the same number of points modulo p (the typical intersection number). The remaining lines with slope d are called renitent. It is sharply (q-λ)-uniform if exactly q-λ lines have the typical intersection number. Let E_λ be a set of (q-λ)-uniform directions with |E_λ| ≤ q.
global_context.target_theorem: Take a multiset T of AG(2,q) and an integer 0 < λ ≤ (q-1)/2. Let E_λ denote a set of (q-λ)-uniform directions of size at most q. The renitent lines with slope in E_λ are contained in an algebraic envelope of class λ^2. Furthermore, if a direction is (q-λ)-uniform, but not sharply uniform, then the line pencil centered at that direction is fully contained in the envelope.

Also, $S(d)=\det H(d)$ and $S_i(d)=\det H_i(d)$ for $(1:d:0)\in \cE_{\lambda}$. Consider the affine curve $\cC$ of degree at most $\lambda^2$ defined by the equation \begin{equation} \label{revX} f(U,V):= S(V)U^\lambda-S_1(V)U^{\lambda-1}-S_2(V) U^{\lambda-2}-\ldots-S_{\lambda-1}(V)U- S_{\lambda}(V). \end{equation}

By Lemma \ref{matrixos}, if $(1:d:0)\in \cE_{\lambda}$ is sharply $(q-\lambda)$-uniform, then the determinant of $H(d)$ is $(-1)^{\lambda(\lambda-1)/2}\prod_{i=1}^{\lambda} c_i(d) \prod_{1 \leq i < j \leq \lambda} (\alpha_i(d)-\alpha_j(d))^2$ and hence $H(d)$ is invertible and the existence of a sharply $(q-\lambda)$-uniform direction ensures that $S(V)$ is not the zero-polynomial. Then by Cramer's rule $(-1)^{i+1}\sigma_i(d) = \det H_i(d) / \det H(d)=S_i(d)/S(d)$ for $1\leq i \leq \lambda$.

Hence if $(1:d:0) \in \cE_{\lambda}$ is sharply $(q-\lambda)$-uniform (that is $\lambda_d = \lambda$), then $(\alpha_i(d),d)$ is a point of $\cC$ defined in \eqref{revX} for each $1\leq i \leq \lambda_d$, since in this case $f(U,d)$ equals \[S(d)(U^{\lambda}-\sigma_1(d)U^{\lambda-1}+\sigma_2(d) U^{\lambda-2}-\ldots+(-1)^{\lambda-1}\sigma_{\lambda-1}(d)U+(-1)^{\lambda}\sigma_{\lambda}(d))=\] \[S(d)\prod_{i=1}^{\lambda}(U-\alpha_i(d)).\] Now consider $(1:d:0)\in \cE_{\lambda}$ such that $\lambda_d < \lambda$. To show that the pencil with carrier $(d)$ is contained with multiplicity $(\lambda-\lambda_d)$ in the envelope, it is enough to prove that $(V-d)^{\lambda-\lambda_d}$ divides $f(U,V)$. (If $(d)$ is a sharply $(q-\lambda)$-uniform direction then $S(d)\neq 0$ and this shows that $V-d$ cannot divide $f(U,V)$, so in this case the pencil with carrier $(d)$ cannot be contained in the envelope.) To do this, we will show \[(V-d)^{\lambda-\lambda_d} \mid S(V),S_1(V), S_2(V),\ldots,S_{\lambda}(V).\] For $1\leq k \leq \lambda$ put $H(d)^{(k)}$ to denote the $k$-th column of $H(d)$. For the integer $k$, $1 \leq k \leq \lambda-\lambda_d $\ , by \eqref{main} applied for the integers $j$ where $\lambda-k-\lambda_d\leq j \leq 2\lambda-k-\lambda_d-1$, in $H(d)$ we obtain that $H(d)^{(k)}$ is the linear combination of $H(d)^{(k+1)},H(d)^{(k+2)},\ldots,H(d)^{(k+\lambda_d)}$. Hence the column space of $H(d)$ is generated by the last $\lambda_d$ columns of $H(d)$, so the rank of this matrix is at most $\lambda_d$. By \eqref{mainmatrix}, the column $C(d)$ is the linear combination of the columns of $H(d)$ and hence when $H(d)$ has rank at most $\lambda_d$, then so is $H_i(d)$ for each $i$. Then for $s>\lambda_d$ the $s\times s$ minors of $H(d)$ have determinant zero. Using Lemma 2.3 of \cite{ZsW} it follows that $d$ is a $(\lambda-\lambda_d)$-fold root of $\det H(d)=S(d)$ and of $\det H_i(d)=S_i(d)$ as we claimed.

paper.title: Splines on Cayley Graphs of the Symmetric Group

global_context.setup: Let Γ be a connected simple graph on vertex set [n], edges identified with transpositions in S_n. The Cayley graph Cay(S_n, E(Γ)) is edge-labeled by ideals (t_i - t_j) for each edge {i,j} in Γ. The ring of splines M_Γ consists of functions f: S_n → C[t_1,...,t_n] such that for each edge (w, w(i j)), f(w) - f(w(i j)) ∈ (t_{w(i)} - t_{w(j)}). This generalizes GKM constructions for equivariant cohomology of flag varieties, Hessenberg varieties, and permutohedral varieties.
global_context.target_theorem: \begin{customthm}{C}\label{intthm:connectedness} Let $\Gamma$ be a connected simple graph. The following are equivalent: \begin{itemize} \item[1)] The graph $\Gamma$ is $k$-connected. \item[2)] For all $d<k$, the symmetric function $\lrep{\Gamma}_d$ corresponds to a trivial representation. \item[3)] For all $d<k$, the $d$-th graded piece of $\splines{\Gamma}$ is isomorphic to the $d$-th graded piece of $\splines{K_n}$, where $K_n$ is the complete graph on $n$ vertices. \end{itemize} \end{customthm}

paper.title: Independent sets in hypergraphs with a forbidden link

global_context.setup: Let $r(H_1,H_2)$ denote the Ramsey number for hypergraphs: the smallest $N$ such that every red/blue coloring of the edges of the complete $k$-graph on $N$ vertices contains a red copy of $H_1$ or a blue copy of $H_2$. For $k=3$, let $K_4^{(3)}\setminus e$ be the 3-uniform hypergraph on four vertices with exactly three edges (i.e., at most two edges among any four vertices). The Erd\H{o}s-Hajnal problem asks for the growth rate of $r(K_4^{(3)}\setminus e, K_n^{(3)})$.
global_context.target_theorem: $r(K_4^{(3)} \setminus e, K_n^{(3)}) = n^{\Theta(n)}$.

local_context: Ramsey theory consists of many deep results across diverse fields of mathematics that can be summarized by the motto ``every large system contains a well-organized subsystem." Well-known examples include Dvoretzky's theorem in Banach space theory, the Paris-Harrington theorem in logic, the Hales-Jewett theorem in combinatorics, Szemer\'edi's theorem in number theory, and the equivalent Furstenberg multiple recurrence theorem in ergodic theory.

The quintessential example, from which the area gets its name, is Ramsey's theorem. If $k\ge 2$, a $k$-uniform hypergraph, or simply $k$-graph, $G=(V,E)$ consists of a vertex set $V$ and an edge set $E\subseteq {V \choose k}$. Ramsey's theorem states that for any $k$-graphs $H_1$ and $H_2$, there is a positive integer $N$ such that any $k$-graph $G$ of order $N$ contains $H_1$ (as a subgraph) or its complement $\overline{G}$ contains $H_2$. The Ramsey number $r(H_1, H_2)$ is the smallest such $N$, and the main goal of graph Ramsey theory is to estimate $r(H_1,H_2)$, especially when one or both of $H_i$ is a complete graph $K_n$.

Even for the graph case $k=2$, the growth rate of Ramsey numbers is not well understood. The best known bounds on diagonal Ramsey numbers are of the form \[ \sqrt{2}^{(1+o(1))n}\le r(K_n,K_n) \le 4^{(1+o(1))n} \] and the exponential constants have not been improved since 1947; see~\cite{Co} and~\cite{Sp} for the best known bounds.

paper.title: Weakly saturated random graphs

global_context.setup: Let $\G_{n,p}$ be the Erdős–Rényi random graph on $n$ vertices with edge probability $p$. For a graph $H$, weak $H$-saturation is defined via the iterative process of adding edges that complete copies of $H$ minus an edge. The critical threshold $p_c(n,H)$ is the infimum $p$ such that $\P(\l\G_{n,p}\r_H = K_n) \ge 1/2$.
global_context.target_theorem: \begin{thm}\label{C_Hmain} If $H$ is balanced (see \cref{D_bal}) then $p_c(n,H)=n^{-1/\lambda+o(1)}$. \end{thm}

local_context: The concept of {\it weak saturation} in graphs was introduced by Bollob{\'a}s \cite{B67}. Given graphs $G$ and $H$, the graph $\l G\r_H$ is obtained by iteratively completing copies of $H$ minus an edge, starting with $G$. Formally, set $G_0=G$, and for $t\ge1$, construct $G_t$ by adding every edge not in $G_{t-1}$ which if added to $G_{t-1}$ creates a new copy of $H$. We let $\l G\r_H=\bigcup_{t} G_t$ denote the result of this procedure. If $\l G\r_H$ is the complete graph on the vertex set of $G$, that is, if all missing edges are eventually added, we say that $G$ is {\it weakly $H$-saturated,} or that it {\it $H$-percolates}.

This process can be viewed as a type of cellular automaton \cite{U50,vN66}, of which bootstrap percolation (see, e.g., \cite{PRK75,CLR79,vE87,AL88,S92,H03,BBDCM12}) is a well-studied example. Balogh, Bollob{\'a}s and Morris \cite{BBM12} introduced a random process called {\it graph bootstrap percolation,} taking $G$ above to be the Erd{\H{o}}s--R{\'e}nyi \cite{ER59} graph $\Gnp$. The critical point $p_c$, at which $\Gnp$ is likely to $H$-percolate, is defined formally as \[ p_c(n, H)=\inf\{p>0:\P(\l\Gnp\r_{H}=K_n)\ge1/2\}. \]

The purpose of this work is to obtain general bounds for $p_c$. Our first main result (see \cref{T_LBgen}) establishes a non-trivial lower bound, which holds for {\it all} $H$. This follows by a general extremal result (see \cref{P_lamstar}) that lower bounds the number of edges in so-called ``witness graphs,'' which add a given edge. This extends the bound in \cite{BBM12}, proved in the case that $H=K_r$, to all $H$. As an application (see \cref{C_Hmain}), we locate $p_c$ up to poly-logarithmic factors for all balanced (see \cref{D_bal}) graphs $H$, partially answering Problem 1 in \cite{BBM12}. Our second main result (see \cref{T_Hmain}) proves a sharper upper bound for all strictly balanced $H$. In particular, this improves the bound in \cite{BBM12} for $K_r$, when $r\ge5$.

The primary focus in \cite{BBM12} is the case that $H=K_r$ is a complete graph (although some other graphs are also analyzed, see \cite[Section 5]{BBM12}). Note that all graphs $K_2$-percolate (any missing edge is added at time $t=1$) so trivially $p_c(n,K_2)=0$. A graph $K_3$-percolates if and only if it is connected, so it follows
x.anchor_hint: A graph $K_3$-percolates if and only if it is connected, so it follows.

paper.title: Pruned inside-out polytopes, combinatorial reciprocity theorems and generalized permutahedra

global_context.setup: The paper introduces pruned inside-out polytopes, a generalization of inside-out polytopes of Beck and Zaslavsky, to study integer point counts via Ehrhart theory. Generalized permutahedra are polytopes in ℝ^d whose normal fan coarsens the braid arrangement fan. For such a polytope 𝒫 and k=0,…,d-1, define χ_{d,k}(𝒫)(m) as the number of integer vectors y in [m]^d such that the y-maximum face of 𝒫 is a k-face.
global_context.target_theorem: For a generalized permutahedron 𝒫 ⊂ ℝ^d and k=0,…,d-1, χ_{d,k}(𝒫)(m) ≔ #{ y ∈ [m]^d : y-maximum face 𝒫_y is a k-face } agrees with a polynomial of degree d−k, and (−1)^{d−k} χ_{d,k}(𝒫)(−m) = ∑_{y∈[m]^d} #{ k-faces of 𝒫_y }.

local_context: \Big)\Big)}\\ &=\#\Big(\Big(\biguplus_{\substack{N\in\N(\Pol)\\ \dim N=d-k}} N^\circ \cap (0,m+1)^d\Big) \cap \Z^d \Big)\,.
\end{split} \end{equation} The intersection $N^\circ \cap (0,m+1)^d$ is the relative interior of a polytope.
Moreover, since $N^\circ$ is an open cone containing the origin, $N^\circ \cap (0,m+1)^d$ is the $(m+1)$\textsuperscript{st} dilate of $N^\circ \cap (0,1)^d$.
Hence, \begin{equation}\label{eq:sumofehrkdim}

\chi_{d,k}(\Pol)(m) =\#\Big(\Big(\biguplus_{\substack{N\in\N(\Pol)\\ \dim N=d-k}} N^\circ \cap (0,m+1)^d\Big) \cap \Z^d \Big) = \sum_{\substack{N\in\N(\Pol)\\ \dim N=d-k}} \ehr_{N^\circ\cap(0,1)^d}(m+1)\,. \end{equation}
x.anchor_hint: Ehrhart's theorem ensures the integer point count in a rational polytope is a polynomial, used here to prove polynomiality of the counting function.

y.reference_tool_latex: For a rational polytope $P  ubset \mathbb{R}^d$, the function $t \mapsto |tP \cap \mathbb{Z}^d|$ is a quasi-polynomial in $t$ of degree $\dim P$. If $P$ is a lattice polytope, it is a polynomial.

paper.title: Coarse cops and robber in graphs and groups

global_context.setup: The paper studies two variants of the cops and robber game on infinite graphs: the strong game and the weak game, defining the strong cop number sco(G) and weak cop number wco(G). In both games, cops and robber move at finite speeds s_c and s_r, with a capture radius ρ and a ball radius R chosen by the robber. The order of choosing parameters differs: in the strong game, s_c, s_r, ρ, R; in the weak game, s_c, ρ, s_r, R. The cop numbers are the minimal number of cops required to win.
global_context.target_theorem: For any connected graph G, sco(G)=1 if and only if G is Gromov-hyperbolic. Consequently, for any finitely generated group Γ, sco(Γ)=1 if and only if Γ is hyperbolic.

paper.title: Grid Induced Minor Theorem for Graphs of Small Degree

global_context.setup: Graphs with maximum degree at most d and treewidth at least f(k,d). The goal is to show the existence of a k×k-grid as an induced minor.
global_context.target_theorem: There is a function f(k, d) = O(k^{10} + 2^{d^5}) so that for any positive integers k,d it holds that if a graph has treewidth at least f(k, d) and maximum degree at most d, then it contains a k × k-grid as an induced minor.

paper.title: Weak Saturation of Multipartite Hypergraphs

global_context.target_theorem: For all $d\geq q\geq 2$, $\nvec$ and $\rvec$ we have $$\mwsat (K^q_{\nvec}, K^q_{\rvec}) = \sum_{I\in \binom{[d]}{q}}\prod_{i\in I}n_i - \sum_{I\in \binom{[d]}{\leq q}} \prod_{i\in I}(n_i-r_i).$$

local_context: For the first statement, suppose that $G\subseteq K_{n,n}$ is weakly $H$-saturated in $K_{n,n}$. Placing two $|V(H)|$-cliques on the parts of $G$ is easily seen to produce a weakly $H$-saturated graph in $K_{2n}$. Therefore, \begin{equation}\label{e:biptrick1} \wsat(2n,H)\leq \wsat(K_{n,n},H)+|V(H)|^2. \end{equation} Conversely, suppose that $G=G_0$ is weakly $H$-saturated in $K_{[n]}$ via a saturating sequence $e_1=\{i_1,j_1\},\dots, e_k=\{i_k,j_k\}$. For $1\leq \ell\leq k$ let $G_\ell=G_0\cup \{e_1,\dots e_\ell\}$, and let $H_\ell$ be a copy of $H$ in $G_\ell$ containing $e_\ell$.

Let $G^{bip}=G\times K_{[2]}$, i.e., $V(G^{bip})=[n]\times \{1,2\}$ and $$E(G^{bip})=\{\{(i,1),(j,2)\}: \{i,j\}\in E(G)\}.$$ We claim that $G^{bip}$ is weakly $H$-saturated in $K_{[n]}^{bip}=K_{[n]}\times K_{[2]}$ via the $H$-saturating sequence \newline $f_1, f'_1, \dots, f_k,f'_k$, where, for each $\ell \in [k]$, $f_\ell=\{(i_\ell,1),(j_\ell,2)\}$ and $f'_\ell=\{(i_\ell,2),(j_\ell,1)\}$, and that $G_{\ell-1}^{bip}\cup \{f_\ell,f'_\ell\}=G_{\ell}^{bip}$ for all $\ell\in [k]$ (where $G_{\ell}^{bip}$ is defined analogously, i.e., $G_{\ell}^{bip}=G_{\ell}\times K_{[2]}$). Indeed, let $(A,B)$ be a bipartition of $V(H_\ell)$ with $i_\ell\in A$ and $j_\ell \in B$, and consider the analogous graph $H^b_\ell$ between $A\times \{1\}$ and $B\times \{2\}$, i.e., for every $(i,j)\in A\times B$ we have $\{(i,1),(j,2)\}\in E(H^b_\ell)$ if and only if $\{i,j\}\in E(H_\ell)$. Note that $f_\ell\in E(H^b_\ell)$ is the only edge of $H^b_\ell$ not already present in $G_{\ell-1}^{bip}$, therefore we can add it to the latter creating a new copy of $H$, namely $H^b_\ell$. Symmetrically, taking a graph $H'^b_\ell$ between $A\times \{2\}$ and $B\times \{1\}$ allows to add $f'_\ell$. Since $G_\ell=G_{\ell-1}\cup e_\ell$, we have $G_{\ell-1}^{bip}\cup \{f_\ell,f'_\ell\}=G_{\ell}^{bip}$. Finally, note that $G^{bip}\cup \{f_1,\dots,f'_k\}=G_k^{bip}=K_{[n]}^{bip}$.

Note that $K_{[n]}^{bip}$ is isomorphic to $K_{n,n}$ minus a perfect matching, and it is a straightforward check that this graph is $H$-saturated in $K_{n,n}$ (we can assume that $|V(H)|\leq n$). We have thus shown \begin{equation}\label{e:biptrick2} \wsat(K_{n,n},H)\leq 2 \wsat(n,H). \end{equation} Combining~\eqref{e:biptrick1} and~\eqref{e:biptrick2} gives $$\frac{\wsat(2n,H)}{2n}-o(1)\leq \frac{\wsat(K_{n,n},H)}{2n}\leq \frac{\wsat(n,H)}{n}, $$ and taking the limit,~\eqref{e:limconst} follows readily.

For the second statement, denote $H=K^{d}_{r_1,\dots,r_d}$ where $1\leq r_1\leq\dots\leq r_d$. Observe that the upper bound in~\eqref{e:hypreduction} holds by Lemma~\ref{l:co-degree}, as $\delta^*(H)=r_1$. To prove the lower bound, suppose $G$ is weakly $H$-saturated in $K^{d}_{[n]}$, and that $|E(G)|=\wsat(n,H)$ . Let $G^{mult}=G\times K_{[d]}^{d}$, that is, $V(G^{mult})=[n]\times [d]$ and $$E(G^{mult})=\{\{(i_1,1),\dots, (i_d,d)\}:\{i_1,\dots, i_d\} \in E(G)\}. $$

Essentially the same argument as for $G^{bip}$ before shows that $G^{mult}$ is weakly $H$-saturated in $K^{d}_{[n]}\times K_{[d]}^{d}$. By Lemma~\ref{l:tensorpartite} adding further $O_H(n^{d-2})$ edges creates a weakly
x.anchor_hint: (n,H)+O(n^ d-2 ). On the other hand, Moshkovitz and Shapira .

z.citation_content: G.~Moshkovitz and A.~Shapira. \newblock Exact bounds for some hypergraph saturation problems. \newblock \em J. Combin. Theory Ser. B, 111:242--248, 2015.
z.locator: $H$-saturated $d$-graph in $K^d(n;d)$. Hence, \begin{equation}\label{eq:multitensor} \wsat(K^{d}(n;d),H) \leq |E(G^{mult})|+O(n^{d-2}) = d! \wsat(n,H)+O(n^{d-2}). \end{equation} On the other hand, Moshkovitz and Shapira~\cite{MoshkShap} proved that $\wsat(K^{d}(n;d),H)=d(r_1-1)n^{d-1}+O(n^{d-2})$. Combining this with~\eqref{eq:multitensor} yields the lower bound in~\eqref{e:hypreduction}.
